Some agencies in this range deliver real results. Most do not. Math: at $1,500 per month for 12 months, you spend $18,000 total. Many agencies at this tier have still not delivered top 3 rankings by month 12. $3,000 to $7,500 per month Mid-market agencies. The work scales up. Usually 6 to 10 content pieces a month, active backlink outreach, technical SEO, dedicated account manager. Results improve but the overhead also climbs: account managers, weekly reports, monthly calls, sales staff. A meaningful share of your retainer pays for the agency's overhead, not the work on your site. Math: $5,000 per month for 12 months is $60,000. That is a serious investment. The results should be too. Often they are not, because the work is spread thin across too many client accounts. $10,000+ per month Enterprise SEO. Multiple dedicated specialists, technical SEO engineers, content teams, link-building outreach, conversion rate optimisation. Real strategy. Usually delivers, but the cost structure assumes the client has hundreds of thousands of dollars annually to spend on SEO. Math: $15,000 per month is $180,000 per year. Worth it for companies whose organic channel drives millions in revenue. Massively overpriced for a business doing $1 to $5 million in revenue. Project-based pricing Some agencies offer fixed-price projects instead of retainers. The work is scoped, the price is set, the engagement ends when the work is done. Project pricing typically ranges from $3,000 to $25,000 depending on scope. Common projects: SEO audit only: $1,500 to $5,000. Reveals problems, gives you a list to fix. Implementation not included. SEO audit plus implementation: $5,000 to $15,000. Audit plus the technical and on-page fixes. SEO migration: $5,000 to $20,000. When you are launching a new site or moving domains. Content-led SEO project: $8,000 to $25,000. Several months of content production targeting agreed keywords. Project pricing is usually a better deal than retainer pricing when the work is well scoped. The downside is that ongoing maintenance is not included, so rankings can drift if no one is watching. Fixed-price outcome-based pricing A small but growing category. The price is fixed, the timeline is fixed, the outcome is agreed in writing. If the outcome is not met, the agency keeps working at no extra cost or refunds part of the fee. Rankday operates this model. Standard $4,900 USD, Growth $7,900 USD, for a 90-day engagement that delivers a new website, top 3 rankings on 90% of agreed keywords, and citations in AI tools like ChatGPT and Perplexity. If the rankings are not delivered by day 90, work continues at no extra cost. The math is interesting. A standard Rankday engagement at $4,900 is equivalent to one month of a $5,000-per-month retainer. But you get a new website (worth $5,000 to $20,000 elsewhere), full SEO work, and AEO work. After 90 days you own everything and you have no ongoing commitment. Quick comparison. At a $1,500-per-month SEO retainer, 12 months costs $18,000. After 12 months you may or may not be ranking. At Rankday's standard plan, 90 days costs $4,900. By day 90 you are ranking for 90% of your agreed keywords or work continues free. Hourly consulting SEO consultants charge $100 to $500 per hour. This model works for specific advice and review work, less so for actual implementation. A 2-hour call can be incredibly valuable. 40 hours of vague consulting often delivers less than a focused project. Use hourly consulting when you have a specific question or want a second opinion. Avoid it as a way to "do SEO" because nobody manages the implementation. Performance / pay-per-rank pricing Some agencies advertise "pay only when you rank." Charges are based on how many keywords you rank for, or how much traffic the rankings deliver. Reality check: this model sounds great in pitch decks. In practice, the keywords that get targeted are usually low-competition long-tails that were going to rank anyway, and the contract terms often have buried clauses about "minimum monthly fees" or "setup costs" that look more like a retainer once you read them carefully. It can work. But it requires careful contract review. The honest version of pay-per-rank is a fixed-price model with a written guarantee, which is what Rankday does. What you should actually pay Forget the model for a second. Work backwards from what the result is worth to you. If a top 3 ranking for your main keyword would bring 50 qualified leads per month, and each lead is worth $2,000 in lifetime value to your business, that ranking is worth $100,000 a month to you. Paying $5,000 a month for SEO to achieve it is rational. Paying $10,000 a month is still rational. If a top 3 ranking would bring 5 qualified leads per month worth $500 each, that ranking is worth $2,500 a month to you. Paying $5,000 a month for SEO is not rational. Paying $500 a month for a freelancer who hits the target is. The correct SEO budget is whatever delivers the outcome at a cost-per-acquisition that beats your other channels. Most businesses pay too much for retainer SEO and not enough for focused, outcome-based execution. Red flags in SEO pricing "We guarantee #1 on Google." Nobody can. Run. "It takes 12 to 18 months to see results." Sometimes true for highly competitive head terms. Often a stalling tactic so the agency can keep billing without showing results. "We need to do 3 months of audit and strategy first." An audit takes a week. Strategy takes another week. Three months of "audit and strategy" is two and a half months of avoiding implementation. "The contract is for 12 months, but we can renegotiate at month 6." Run. "We can't tell you which keywords until we start work." You should be able to agree on a keyword list before any contract is signed. Bottom line The price you should pay depends on what the outcome is worth to you and how the work is structured. The default monthly retainer model usually overcharges relative to the result. Fixed-price outcome-based pricing matches cost to result better. Rankday's pricing is published openly: Standard $4,900 USD, Growth $7,900 USD, both for 90 days of work that includes website, SEO, and AEO. Local pricing in AED and GBP. The deliverables and the guarantee are in writing before anything starts. See the full pricing breakdown. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## How to choose an SEO agency: 12 questions to ask before you sign | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/how-to-choose-an-seo-agency

← All posts Agency How to choose an SEO agency: 12 questions to ask before you sign By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 22, 2026 · 9 min read Most SEO agencies will not survive a serious vetting process. The pitch deck is polished, the case studies are vague, and the contract is built to extract maximum revenue with minimum accountability. If you ask the right questions before signing, you will eliminate 80% of bad agencies in a single call. Here are the 12 questions to ask, and what the right answers look like. The 12 questions 1. Which keywords can you rank me for, and in what timeframe? The right answer involves a specific keyword list with realistic timelines per keyword. A good agency will refuse to commit to keywords without research first, but should commit after a discovery call. Bad answer: "We'll figure that out together once we start." That means they have not done the work to know. 2. What does your guarantee look like in writing? Most agencies will not give you a written guarantee. The few that do usually limit it to "if we don't make progress in 6 months, we'll work for free for one month." That is not a real guarantee. A real guarantee specifies: which keywords, what position, by what date, and what happens if the target is missed. Rankday's version: top 3 on 90% of agreed keywords by day 90, or work continues at no extra cost. 3. How is the engagement structured, week by week? Ask for a week-by-week plan. The agency should be able to walk you through what happens in week 1, week 4, week 8, and week 12. Vague answers like "every project is different" or "we tailor our approach" usually mean there is no structured playbook. A good agency has done this before and knows what week 6 looks like. 4. Do you do paid advertising too? This question is about focus. Agencies that do everything (SEO, paid ads, social media, email, content, web design) often do nothing well. They are revenue-maximising businesses, not SEO specialists. The best SEO agencies focus narrowly. They will say "we do SEO and AEO. For paid ads, here's a paid agency we trust." That is the right answer. 5. Who actually does the work? Many agencies pitch with senior staff and execute with juniors or offshore contractors. Ask specifically: who is writing my content, who is doing outreach, who is doing technical SEO. Get names. Then in the contract, ask if those specific people will be assigned to your account. If they cannot commit, expect the work to be done by whoever has bandwidth that month. 6. How do you handle keyword scope creep? Halfway through an engagement, you will think of more keywords you want to rank for. A bad agency will say yes to all of them, then deliver on none. A good agency has a written policy: the keyword list is agreed and fixed at the start. New keywords can be added, but only by scoping a new engagement or extending the timeline. Boundaries protect both sides. 7. What's your approach to backlinks? Backlinks are the single biggest off-page ranking factor. The wrong approach to them can permanently damage your domain. Bad answers: "We have a network of high-DA sites we can place links on." (Link farm.) "We use guest post marketplaces." (Spam.) "We don't do backlinks." (Then they will not rank you for competitive keywords.) Good answer: a mix of editorial outreach to relevant industry publications, citations on credible directories, and earned mentions through PR and content. Slow but durable. 8. How do you report progress? Many agencies bury weak progress under a 40-slide monthly report. Lots of graphs, no answers to "are we ranking yet?" Good agencies give you a simple weekly or fortnightly update: which keywords moved this period, which content went live, which citations were earned. If the report cannot fit in a Loom video or a one-page document, the agency is hiding something. 9. Can I see your client list? Most agencies will share 2 to 5 named clients as case studies. Ask for the full list. The full list often reveals churn, niche fit, or whether they have ever worked in your industry. Some agencies have NDAs that prevent this. Reasonable. But they should be able to share anonymised metrics: average client engagement length, retention rate, average ranking outcomes. 10. What happens if I want to leave? Read the contract before you sign. Look for: auto-renewal clauses, minimum term lengths, cancellation fees, ownership of content created during the engagement. If the contract makes leaving expensive, the agency is structured to retain you whether or not they perform. A good agency makes leaving easy because they expect you to stay on merit. 11. Who owns the website and content at the end? Some agencies retain ownership of the content they create, or build websites on proprietary platforms you cannot take with you. That is hostage architecture. Confirm: domain, content, code, accounts (Google, GA4, Search Console, GBP). All in your name. All yours when you leave. Anything less is a red flag. 12. Do you do AEO too, or just SEO? AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) is what gets you cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Claude. Most SEO agencies in 2026 do not do AEO at all. They are still optimising for Google rankings alone. AEO is increasingly the difference between getting found and not getting found. An agency that does not even discuss AEO is selling a product designed for 2018, not 2026. Red flags that should disqualify any agency Guarantees of #1 on Google. Nobody can guarantee this. Anyone who does is lying. Pressure to sign at the end of the discovery call. Real agencies do not need to close in one meeting. Vague case studies with no metrics. "We helped Client X grow their traffic" is meaningless without numbers. "We can't show you our process." Process secrecy is usually a cover for not having one. Contract terms hidden until signing. Ask for the contract during the discovery call. Read it before any meeting where you might commit. If an agency answers more than 3 of these 12 questions vaguely or evasively, do not sign. There are better agencies. The right one will answer all 12 directly without hedging. The shortlist process Here is a workflow that will surface the right agency in 2 to 3 weeks: Build a shortlist of 5 to 7 agencies. Use Clutch, GoodFirms, recommendations from peers, and Google searches for your specific niche. Send each one a short brief explaining your business, your target keywords, and your budget. Ask for a written proposal, not just a call. Eliminate any agency that cannot or will not put a proposal in writing. Run discovery calls with the 3 to 4 that remain. Use the 12 questions above. Get 2 to 3 written proposals with specific keywords, timelines, and pricing. Check references. Call 2 past clients of each shortlisted agency. Read the contract carefully. Negotiate any auto-renewal or hostage clauses. Pick the one whose answers stood up to scrutiny and whose contract is structured to protect both sides. What Rankday's answers look like Since this is the Rankday blog, here is how we answer the same 12 questions. Keywords and timeline: agreed in week 1 with you, fixed in writing, top 3 by day 90. Guarantee: 90% of agreed keywords in top 3 by day 90, or work continues at no extra cost. Week by week: documented on the how it works page. Paid ads: we do not. We refer to specialists if you need them. Who does the work: small team. You work with the people doing the work, not an account manager. Scope creep: keyword list fixed at start. Additions go into a new engagement. Backlinks: editorial outreach and citation network only. No farms. Reporting: short weekly Loom update. No 40-page reports. Clients: happy to introduce you to past clients on request. Leaving: no contract, no auto-renewal. Once 90 days end, the engagement ends unless you opt into maintenance. Ownership: domain, content, code, accounts. All yours. Always. AEO: included in every engagement. Not an add-on. If those answers fit what you are looking for, see the pricing or message us on WhatsApp via the FAQ page. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## How to optimise for Google AI Overviews | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/how-to-optimise-for-google-ai-overviews

← All posts AEO / GEO How to optimise for Google AI Overviews By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 8 min read Google AI Overviews now appear above traditional search results for a growing share of queries. They get clicks before organic listings do. Many websites have already lost meaningful organic traffic to them. Here is how to get your content pulled into AI Overviews, and how to recover traffic if you have already lost it. What Google AI Overviews are AI Overviews are AI-generated summary answers that appear at the top of Google search results for certain queries. They pull from indexed web content and synthesise it into a single paragraph (or sometimes a structured list) that directly answers the user's question. Below the Overview, traditional organic results still appear. Users can scroll past the Overview to see them, but many users do not. Click-through rates on top organic results have dropped 30 to 60% on queries where Overviews appear. AI Overviews are not consistent across queries. Some queries trigger an Overview every time. Some never. Some trigger it for some users and not others. Google's internal logic decides when an Overview adds value. Which queries trigger AI Overviews Based on observation since the feature rolled out: Informational queries: "how does X work", "what is X", "why does X happen". Almost always trigger Overviews. Comparison queries: "X vs Y", "difference between X and Y". Frequently trigger Overviews. List queries: "best X", "top X for Y", "X compared". Sometimes trigger Overviews with structured lists. How-to queries: "how to do X". Often trigger Overviews with step-by-step responses. Transactional queries: "buy X near me", "X price". Rarely trigger Overviews. Google still favours local pack and shopping results here. Brand or navigational queries: "Acme Corp website". Rarely trigger Overviews. How Google AI Overviews choose source content Google has not published a complete spec, but the signals are roughly: Content that already ranks well. Sources for Overviews are usually pulled from the top 10 organic results, sometimes the top 20. Content that directly answers the question. Pages structured as Q&A or with clear topic headings get pulled more often than narrative articles. Content with high authority. Domains with strong backlink profiles and editorial reputation are sourced more often. Content with structured data. Schema markup (especially FAQPage, HowTo, Article) signals what a page is about. Content that is current. Recently updated pages are favoured for queries where freshness matters. Content with author attribution. Pages with named authors and credentials get pulled more often than anonymous content. How to optimise your content for AI Overviews 1. Structure for direct answers Open each section with a 1 to 3 sentence direct answer to a specific question. Follow with the details. Bad: a 4-paragraph narrative leading to an implicit answer. Good: "[Direct answer in one sentence]. [Two to three sentences of qualification.]" Then the supporting detail. AI Overviews can lift that opening sentence cleanly. Narrative-style content is harder to extract. 2. Use question-shaped headings H2s and H3s formatted as questions ("What is X?", "How does X work?", "Why does X matter?") match query patterns and get pulled into Overviews more often. Generic headings like "Overview" or "About" do not. 3. Apply FAQPage and HowTo schema FAQPage schema marks up question-answer pairs as structured data. HowTo schema marks up step-by-step instructions. Both are heavily used by Google AI Overviews. Pages with these schemas are pulled more frequently than equivalent content without them. 4. Write concise, declarative sentences AI Overviews favour content that can be lifted as standalone statements. Long, hedged, qualifier-heavy prose is harder to extract. Compare: Hard to lift: "While it may depend on various factors, generally speaking, SEO can take a considerable amount of time before measurable results become apparent in most cases." Easy to lift: "SEO typically takes 60 to 120 days to produce first rankings, and 6 to 12 months for competitive keywords." Both might be technically true. The second gets pulled into Overviews. 5. Include concrete data and specific numbers Specific facts get lifted. Vague claims do not. "Our process is fast" is not Overview-friendly. "Our 90-day process delivers top 3 rankings on 90% of agreed keywords" is. 6. Add author bylines and credentials Article schema with a named author, plus an author bio on the page with credentials, signals to Google that the content has a real expert behind it. Particularly important for YMYL (Your Money, Your Life) topics like health, finance, and legal. 7. Keep content current Add datePublished and dateModified schema. Update old content with refreshed data, current examples, and revised dates. Google favours recently updated content for queries where freshness matters. 8. Earn external citations AI Overviews often cite multiple sources. Content from domains with strong backlink profiles gets included more often. Backlink work for traditional SEO also helps for Overview inclusion. Test: search a target informational query on Google. If an AI Overview appears, note which sources it cites. Those are your direct competitors for that query. Study how they structure the answers Google chose to lift. How to recover traffic if AI Overviews are eating it If your traffic has dropped on queries where Overviews now appear, you have three options. Option 1: become the source Restructure your existing content to be more Overview-friendly using the techniques above. Aim to be the source the Overview cites. Even when Overview clicks are lower than traditional organic clicks, being cited as a source builds brand recognition and drives some clickthrough from users who want more depth. Option 2: shift to queries that don't trigger Overviews Transactional and brand queries rarely trigger AI Overviews. Shift your content strategy toward queries with commercial intent and away from purely informational queries. "Best SEO agency Dubai" (transactional) is more valuable than "how does SEO work" (informational) for a service business anyway. Optimise toward the queries that convert, not just the ones that drive traffic. Option 3: build alternative channels Google traffic is no longer the only path. Direct traffic, email, social, AI engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude), and YouTube all provide alternatives. For service businesses, building visibility on multiple channels reduces the risk of any single algorithm change destroying your pipeline. What this means strategically Google AI Overviews are part of a larger shift. The search results page is becoming more AI-driven. Informational queries are increasingly resolved at the top of the page without organic clicks. Transactional and commercial queries still drive traffic, but the path is more competitive. For service businesses like yours, the practical response is: Optimise existing content for Overview inclusion (structure, schema, freshness) Prioritise commercial-intent keywords over purely informational ones Build visibility in AI engines beyond Google (Perplexity, ChatGPT, Claude) Diversify traffic sources so no single channel determines the business outcome What Rankday does Every page we publish is optimised for both traditional Google rankings and AI Overview inclusion. Schema markup, question-shaped structure, concrete data, author attribution, and freshness signals are all applied by default. We also track AI Overview citations weekly during the engagement, so you know whether your content is being pulled. See our full approach to AI citation. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## How to write meta titles and descriptions that actually get clicks | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/how-to-write-meta-titles-and-descriptions

← All posts SEO How to write meta titles and descriptions that actually get clicks By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 8 min read Your meta title and description are your one-shot pitch to a searcher. The page might be brilliant, but if the SERP snippet does not earn the click, nobody reads it. Most sites waste these fields on auto-generated junk. Here is how to write titles and descriptions that get clicks, with patterns that work and patterns that do not. Why these fields matter more than people think Two reasons: Click-through rate (CTR) is a ranking factor. Google watches whether users click your result. Low CTR signals weak relevance and pushes you down. The snippet is your sales pitch. You won the impression. Now you have to win the click against 9 other results above and below you. A page ranked #5 with a 12% CTR can drive more traffic than a page ranked #3 with a 3% CTR. The snippet is the difference. Meta title: the rules 1. Length: 50 to 60 characters Google truncates titles around 60 characters (about 580 pixels). Aim for 50 to 60. Shorter is fine. Longer gets cut off mid-sentence. 2. Primary keyword near the beginning Google weights the first words of the title more heavily. Put your target keyword in the first half. Bad: "Discover Rankday: The leading SEO agency in Dubai" (keyword too late) Good: "SEO Agency Dubai. Top 3 Rankings in 90 Days. | Rankday" (keyword first) 3. One unique title per page Never duplicate titles across pages. Each page needs its own title that distinguishes it. 4. Brand name at the end (or omit it) Putting your brand name at the end keeps the keyword visible in truncated SERPs. Putting it at the beginning sometimes makes sense for established brands but usually wastes pixels. 5. Specific and quantified Numbers, dates, and specifics increase clicks. "10 best CRMs" outperforms "best CRMs." "90-Day SEO" outperforms "SEO Services." 6. Match search intent A buyer searching "best SEO agency UAE" wants comparison or recommendation. A title saying "Our SEO Services" misses the intent. A title saying "Best SEO Agencies in UAE: Top 5 Compared" matches it. Meta description: the rules 1. Length: 150 to 160 characters Google truncates around 155 to 160 characters. Aim for 150 to 160. 2. Include the keyword (Google bolds matches) When the searcher's query matches words in your meta description, Google bolds them. Bolded text catches the eye and increases clicks. Include the primary keyword and 1 to 2 related variations naturally. 3. Answer the searcher's implicit question The searcher typed a query. The description should answer "yes, this page solves that problem." Query: "how long does SEO take" Bad description: "We are a professional SEO agency offering data-driven solutions." Good description: "SEO timelines explained for new domains, established sites, and competitive markets. Honest answer for 2026, with breakdowns by scenario." 4. Include a call to action End with a soft CTA: "Read the breakdown." "See the pricing." "Learn how." 5. Avoid duplication and auto-generation Never duplicate descriptions across pages. And never let WordPress, Webflow, or a CMS auto-generate them from the first paragraph. Write each one deliberately. High-converting patterns Pattern 1: Problem + outcome + proof "[Common problem the searcher has]. [Specific outcome you deliver]. [Proof point]." Example: "Stuck below page 1 of Google? Top-3 rankings on agreed keywords in 90 days. 90% success rate or we keep working free." Pattern 2: Direct answer with specifics "[Direct answer to the query]. [Specifics that build credibility]." Example: "SEO costs range from $300 monthly retainers to $20,000+ enterprise contracts. Here is what each price actually buys you in 2026." Pattern 3: Question + answer hook "[Question that matches search intent]? [Hook that promises the answer in the article]." Example: "Why is your website not ranking on Google? Nine most common reasons, with a 30-day diagnostic plan to fix them." Pattern 4: Number + benefit + qualifier "[Number] [benefit] [qualifier that increases credibility]." Example: "12 questions to ask before signing with an SEO agency. Use these to filter out 80% of bad agencies on the first call." Patterns that do not work "Welcome to..." Wastes characters and tells Google nothing useful. "Discover..." Generic verb that no human searches for. "The leading..." Unverifiable superlative. Google does not weight it. "Our..." The page is about the user, not about you. Lead with what they get. Keyword stuffing. "SEO agency Dubai SEO services Dubai SEO Dubai" looks spammy and Google may rewrite the title for you. All caps or excessive symbols. Looks like clickbait. Google often rewrites these. Test before publishing. Mock up the SERP listing in a tool like SERPSim or just on paper. Read it like a stranger seeing it for the first time. Would you click? If the answer is "maybe," rewrite until the answer is "yes." How Google sometimes overrides your title In about 20 to 30% of cases, Google rewrites your meta title in the SERP based on its own assessment of relevance. Common triggers: Title is too long Title is identical to other pages on your site Title contains keyword stuffing Title does not match the actual page content Better signal exists elsewhere on the page (H1 or natural language) If Google is rewriting your titles, rewrite them yourself to match what Google's algorithm chose. Often Google has identified a better title than your original. Title and description for AI search For pages targeted at AI search engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews), meta titles and descriptions matter slightly less because AI engines synthesise from full content. But they still: Signal what the page is primarily about Get included in some AI-generated answers verbatim Provide context to schema markup Optimise for both: clear, specific, keyword-led for Google, and informative enough that AI engines can correctly categorise the page. What Rankday does Every Rankday-built site has unique, keyword-optimised, intent-matched meta titles and descriptions on every page. We write them as part of the launch, not as an afterthought. Each page also includes Open Graph and Twitter Card metadata for social sharing previews. Want examples? Browse any page on rank-day.com and view the source. Each one demonstrates the patterns above. See the pricing or the 90-day breakdown. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Internal linking strategy: how to pass authority between your pages | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/internal-linking-strategy

← All posts SEO Internal linking strategy: how to pass authority between your pages By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 8 min read Internal links tell Google which pages on your site matter most and pass authority between them. Most sites do this badly, accidentally, or not at all. Done well, internal linking can lift rankings by 20 to 40% with no new content needed. Here is a simple strategy that works for sites of any size. Why internal links matter Three reasons: Crawlability. Google discovers new pages by following links. Pages with no inbound internal links are rarely crawled. PageRank flow. Authority passes through links. A page linked from your homepage receives more authority than a page buried 4 clicks deep. Topical signals. When multiple related pages link to each other, Google understands them as a topic cluster, which builds topical authority faster. The hub-and-spoke model The cleanest internal linking strategy for most sites is a hub-and-spoke model. It looks like this: Homepage: the top-level hub. Links to major sections. Category hubs: pages that group related content (e.g., /industries, /locations, /blog). Each links to all the specific pages in its category. Spoke pages: the specific service, location, blog post, or product pages. Each links back to its hub and across to related spokes. Visualised, it looks like a wheel: hub in the middle, spokes pointing outward, with connections between adjacent spokes. Practical rules 1. Every page should have inbound internal links Run a crawler (Screaming Frog free up to 500 URLs). Look at the "orphan pages" report. Any page with zero inbound internal links is invisible to Google's authority calculation. Fix: add the orphan page to the relevant hub, or link to it from related pages. 2. Important pages need many internal links Your highest-priority pages (homepage, pricing, top service pages) should be linked from many places: nav, footer, related content, and contextual mentions in body copy. Rule of thumb: pages you want to rank highly should have 10+ internal links pointing to them from across the site. 3. Use descriptive anchor text The clickable text of the link tells Google what the linked page is about. Bad: "Click here to read more." Good: "See our SEO pricing for Dubai businesses." Use natural, descriptive anchor text. Vary it so it does not look manipulated. Avoid using the same exact-match keyword anchor 50 times. 4. Link to related pages contextually When you mention a topic in body content, link to your dedicated page on it. This is the most powerful kind of internal link. Example: in a blog post about local SEO, when you mention Google Business Profile, link to your guide on GBP setup. 5. Use a clean global nav Your top nav (and footer) should link to the most important pages. These links appear on every page and pass significant authority. Limit to 5 to 7 nav items. Crowded navs dilute the signal. 6. Link forward and backward in clusters Within a topic cluster (e.g., all your SEO blog posts), each post should link to several related posts. Forward links to deeper or more specific content. Backward links to foundational content. Example: a post about "how to choose an SEO agency" should link forward to "how much does SEO cost" and backward to "what is SEO." 7. Avoid excessive footer links Some sites stuff dozens of links into the footer. Google sees these as low-value because they are site-wide and not contextually relevant. Use the footer for genuine navigation, not link manipulation. How to audit your current internal linking Step 1: Map your current links Use Screaming Frog or Ahrefs Site Audit to crawl your site and export a link map. Identify: Pages with zero internal links pointing to them (orphans) Pages with fewer than 3 internal links (under-linked) Pages with excessive links (over-linked, possibly diluting authority) Internal links using "click here" or other generic anchor text Step 2: Identify your priority pages Which pages do you want to rank highest? Typically: homepage, pricing, top service pages, top blog posts. These should be your "destinations" for most internal linking work. Step 3: Audit category hubs Do you have hub pages for major categories? Examples: /services, /industries, /locations, /blog. If not, create them. Each hub should link to every specific page in its category. Hub pages compound authority for their category significantly. Step 4: Add contextual links Go through your top 10 to 20 most-trafficked pages. Add 2 to 5 contextual internal links per page where they naturally fit. Each link should: Point to a related page on your site Use descriptive anchor text Fit naturally in the surrounding content Add value for the reader (not just SEO-driven) Example: how Rankday's site is internally linked The Rankday site follows a hub-and-spoke model: Homepage links to How It Works, Pricing, Industries, Locations, Blog, FAQ, About Industries hub at /industries links to all 10 industry pages (SaaS, law firms, clinics, plumbers, electricians, contractors, interior designers, fit out, FM, maid services) Locations hub at /locations links to Dubai, UK, US plus 5 other markets Blog hub at /blog links to every blog post Each industry page links back to the Industries hub and to /pricing and /how-it-works Each blog post links contextually to related blog posts and to relevant service pages Footer mirrors the hub structure for site-wide accessibility This creates a network of links where authority flows from the homepage outward to the spokes, and back inward through cross-links between related pages. Quick rule. Before publishing any new page, ask "where on the existing site should this page be linked from?" Add the link before publishing. Otherwise the new page launches as an orphan and ranks slowly. Internal linking and AI search AI engines use internal links similarly to Google. Pages linked from authoritative parts of your site (homepage, key hubs) are weighted more heavily when AI engines parse your site's content. For AEO specifically, internal links between related content help AI engines understand topical relationships. A site where "what is AEO," "how to get cited by ChatGPT," and "what is GEO" all link to each other signals topical coherence that AI engines reward. What Rankday does Internal linking is built into the architecture of every Rankday site from day one. We design the hub-and-spoke structure during the planning phase, ensure every page has inbound and outbound links by launch, and add contextual links across the content as it goes live. The result: no orphan pages, clear hub structure for Google to understand, authority flowing from homepage to priority pages, and topical clusters that compound. See how the 90-day plan handles the build. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Keyword research for small businesses: a practical 30-minute method | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/keyword-research-for-small-business

← All posts SEO Keyword research for small businesses: a practical 30-minute method By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 8 min read Most keyword research advice is built for agencies running enterprise SEO. The workflows assume Ahrefs and Semrush subscriptions, 40-tab spreadsheets, and a content team to act on the results. Small businesses do not need any of that. Here is a 30-minute method that finds the keywords actually worth targeting. No paid tools required. The principle: search intent over search volume Most keyword research focuses on volume: which terms get the most monthly searches. That is the wrong starting point for small businesses. A keyword with 100 monthly searches and high commercial intent is more valuable than a keyword with 10,000 searches and zero buying intent. The 100 searches are people about to spend money. The 10,000 are people doing research who will never become customers. For small businesses, the ranking math is: Ignore: head terms with high volume and high competition Target: long-tail terms with medium volume and clear commercial intent Track: brand and navigational terms (your business name + variations) The 30-minute method Step 1 (5 minutes): list what your buyers actually search Open a blank document. Write down the 20 things a real buyer would type into Google when looking for what you sell. Use real customer language, not your industry jargon. For a dental clinic in Dubai, this might be: dentist near me dental implants Dubai price emergency dentist Dubai orthodontist Dubai dentist Business Bay teeth whitening cost Dubai kids dentist Dubai ... (continue to 20) Tip: ask your front desk or sales person what callers ask for. They hear the actual language buyers use. Step 2 (10 minutes): expand with Google autocomplete Open Google in an incognito window (so personalisation does not skew results). Type each of your 20 base terms, one by one. Note what Google autocompletes. Then scroll to the bottom of the search results page. The "People also ask" and "Related searches" sections give you 10 to 15 more variations per query. For "dental implants Dubai," you might find: dental implants Dubai price dental implants Dubai cost dental implants Dubai review best dental implants in Dubai cheap dental implants Dubai full mouth dental implants Dubai single tooth dental implant Dubai Add the relevant ones to your list. You should now have 50 to 80 keyword candidates. Step 3 (5 minutes): categorise by intent Sort your list into three buckets: Commercial intent: the searcher is ready to spend money. Examples: "dental implants Dubai price," "dentist Business Bay book appointment," "emergency dentist near me." Research intent: the searcher is comparing or learning. Examples: "dental implants vs bridges," "how long do dental implants last," "are dental implants safe." Brand intent: searches for your business name or close variations. Always rank for these. Commercial intent terms are your priority. They convert. Research intent terms are valuable for content marketing and AI citation but secondary. Brand terms should be automatic wins for you and require minimal work. Step 4 (5 minutes): check the competition For each commercial intent term, search it on Google. Look at the top 5 organic results. Ask: Are the top results dominated by enterprise brands or major directories? Are the top results small businesses similar to yours? What format are the top results: blog posts, service pages, product pages, directory listings? If the top 5 are all major brands (e.g., big hospital chains or massive directory sites), the term is too competitive for now. Park it for later. If the top 5 are small businesses similar to yours, the term is rankable. Keep it on the list. Step 5 (5 minutes): finalise your priority list From the original 50 to 80 candidates, you should end with 15 to 25 priority keywords. These are your targets for the next 90 days. Group them by topic. Often you will find 3 to 5 keywords that can be covered by a single high-quality page. For example: "dental implants Dubai price," "dental implants Dubai cost," and "how much do dental implants cost in Dubai" can all be targeted by one well-written service page. At the end of this step, you should have: A list of 15 to 25 priority commercial-intent keywords Grouped into 5 to 10 page topics Each topic has a clear page format (service page, location page, comparison page, etc.) Tools you can use, if you want them The 30-minute method above requires no paid tools. If you want to validate your list with data: Google Keyword Planner (free, requires Google Ads account): shows monthly search volume. Be aware the ranges are broad. Ubersuggest (free tier): rough volume and competition estimates. AnswerThePublic (free tier): visualises questions related to your seed term. Ahrefs / Semrush (paid): the most accurate data, useful if budget allows. For small businesses, the free tools are sufficient. The expensive tools mostly buy you precision that does not change the keyword list materially. How to use your keyword list Now that you have 15 to 25 priority keywords grouped into 5 to 10 topics, here is what to do: Build a dedicated page for each topic, optimised for the keywords in that group Add the primary keyword to the page title, H1, and meta description Mention all keywords from the group naturally in the body, including related questions Internal-link from your homepage and from related pages to the new keyword-targeted page Apply appropriate schema markup (Service, FAQ, LocalBusiness, etc.) Track ranking progress weekly using Google Search Console (free) Realistic timeline. For most small businesses with this approach, 60% of priority keywords will be in the top 10 within 60 to 90 days. Some will be top 3. Some competitive terms will take longer. Stick with the list. Do not change it every two weeks. What about AEO keyword research? For AEO and GEO, the keyword research is slightly different. Instead of focusing on what people type into Google, focus on what people ask conversational AI. Open ChatGPT or Perplexity. Type the conversational version of your target query: "What's the best dental clinic in Dubai for implants?" or "Who does emergency plumbing in JBR Dubai?" See what answer the AI gives. Note which businesses get cited. That gives you two pieces of information: your AEO competitors, and the kinds of attributes the AI mentions (price, location, specialty, reviews, etc.). Optimise your content to surface those attributes prominently. What Rankday does differently At Rankday we run keyword research in week 1 of every engagement. The output is a scoped, written list of 15 to 30 priority keywords (depending on plan) that we both agree on. The list is fixed in writing before any other work starts. Then we deliver against that list. Top 3 rankings on 90% of agreed keywords by day 90, or we keep working at no extra cost. See the full 90-day breakdown. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Mobile-first indexing explained: what Google actually crawls in 2026 | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/mobile-first-indexing-explained

← All posts SEO Mobile-first indexing explained: what Google actually crawls in 2026 By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 8 min read Mobile-first indexing means Google uses the mobile version of your site as the primary version it crawls, indexes, and ranks. If your mobile experience is worse than your desktop experience, Google is judging you on your worst content. Most site owners still think of the desktop experience as the "real" site and mobile as the secondary one. That has been backwards since 2019, and the gap has widened every year since. What mobile-first actually means Until 2019, Google had two crawlers: a desktop crawler that crawled and indexed the desktop version, and a mobile crawler that supplemented it. Desktop was the canonical version. Mobile was the bonus. Since 2019, this has been inverted. The mobile version is the canonical version. Google's main crawler is the smartphone Googlebot. Desktop content is sometimes considered as a supplement but is not the primary signal. Consequences: If a page exists on desktop but is hidden on mobile, Google may not index it If an image exists on desktop but is removed on mobile, Google does not see it If structured data is on desktop but missing on mobile, the schema is missing for Google's purposes If page load is fast on desktop but slow on mobile, Google ranks you based on the slow version How to know if mobile-first is hurting you Symptoms Pages drop in rankings after a Google algorithm update Mobile traffic declines disproportionately compared to desktop Search Console shows pages indexed but ranking poorly Page speed is fine on desktop but bad on mobile Quick diagnostic Open Search Console. Under Pages, look at "Crawl" data. The User-agent column tells you which crawler last visited each page. If it consistently shows "Smartphone" Googlebot, you are on mobile-first indexing (almost all sites are by 2026). Then visit your most important pages on your phone. Compare to desktop. Anything missing, broken, or worse on mobile is something Google is judging you on. Common mobile-first issues 1. Content hidden on mobile Some responsive designs hide certain sections on mobile (sidebars, secondary navigation, "expandable" content). If that content matters for ranking, hiding it costs you. Fix: render the same content on mobile and desktop. Use responsive layout to rearrange, not to remove. 2. Smaller H1s and headings H1 and H2 hierarchy should be identical on mobile and desktop. If your mobile shows the H1 in a small font that visually reads as body text, you may also be marking it up wrong in the HTML. 3. Images at lower quality on mobile Responsive images are good. But if your mobile images are so compressed they lose visual quality and alt text is missing or different, image search ranking suffers. 4. Different structured data Schema markup must be present on mobile, not just desktop. Some templates accidentally inject schema only when desktop CSS rules apply. 5. Slow mobile load times Core Web Vitals are measured separately for mobile. A site that scores 95 on desktop and 45 on mobile is ranked based on the 45. 6. Intrusive interstitials Pop-ups, full-screen overlays, and aggressive cookie banners on mobile are penalised more strictly than on desktop. They block the content the user came to see. 7. Tap targets too small Buttons and links should be at least 48×48 pixels with sufficient spacing. Cramped targets cause accidental taps and fail mobile usability checks. 8. Horizontal scroll Mobile pages should never require horizontal scroll. Test by loading on your phone in portrait. If you have to scroll sideways to read, the layout is broken. Mobile usability tests to run Google Search Console: Mobile Usability report Search Console flags specific mobile issues per page: text too small, clickable elements too close, content wider than screen. Fix each one. PageSpeed Insights mobile score Aim for 90+ on mobile. Anything below 50 is actively hurting rankings. The report tells you which specific assets are slow. Manual phone test Open your top 5 pages on your phone, in cellular network mode (not Wi-Fi). Time the load. Try to tap every button. Read the content. Anything that feels broken to you is broken for users. What good mobile-first design looks like Same content as desktop, never less Fast initial render (LCP under 2.5 seconds on 4G) Single column layout that works on narrow screens without zoom Tap targets at least 48×48 pixels with comfortable spacing Large readable text (16 pixels minimum body font) No horizontal scroll, no zoom required Cookie banner and email opt-in modals minimised on mobile (or absent until users engage) Images responsive with srcset and proper width/height attributes Schema markup applied to mobile, not just desktop Practical exercise. Open your homepage on your phone. Time how long until you can interact (tap something useful, not just see the page). If it is over 3 seconds, you have a mobile speed problem regardless of what Search Console reports. Mobile-first and AI search AI engines like ChatGPT and Perplexity that browse the live web typically pull the version of your site they see when rendering. If your mobile version is the canonical, that is what they see and cite. AEO and GEO inherit the mobile-first reality. A site that renders poorly on mobile gets cited less by AI engines for similar reasons it ranks lower on Google. What Rankday does Every Rankday-built site is designed mobile-first from the start. We test on real phones, target Core Web Vitals scores of 90+ on mobile, and ensure that mobile is the canonical experience, not a degraded version of the desktop site. This is foundational. A site that fails mobile-first cannot rank consistently, no matter how good the content or backlinks are. See the 90-day breakdown for the full technical scope. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Schema markup for beginners: what it is, why it matters, with examples | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/schema-markup-for-beginners

← All posts SEO Schema markup for beginners: what it is, why it matters, with examples By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 9 min read Schema markup is the structured data code embedded in your website that tells search engines and AI engines exactly what your business does, who it serves, and how it works. Most websites have none of it. The ones that do consistently outperform competitors on both Google rich results and AI citations. Here is what schema is, why it matters, and copy-paste examples for the schemas your site probably needs. What schema markup is Schema is a vocabulary developed by Schema.org (a collaboration between Google, Microsoft, Yahoo, and Yandex) for describing web content in a machine-readable way. The most common implementation is JSON-LD: a small block of JSON code embedded in the <head> of a page. Without schema, search engines have to infer what your page is about from prose. With schema, they know explicitly. A simplified example: Without schema: "We are a dental clinic in Dubai offering implants, cleanings, and orthodontics." Google reads this and probably figures it out. Probably. With schema: a structured block of JSON that explicitly says: type = Dentist, name = X, address = Y, services = [Z, A, B], opening hours = C. Google knows exactly what it is looking at. Why schema matters in 2026 1. Rich results on Google Schema enables rich results: enhanced search listings with star ratings, FAQ dropdowns, recipe details, event dates, product prices, and more. Rich results typically get 30 to 100% higher click-through rates than standard blue-link results. 2. AI engine understanding ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Google AI Overviews all parse structured data to understand what businesses do. Schema is one of the strongest signals an AI engine has for confidently citing a business in an answer. 3. Knowledge Graph entry For brands, schema is a key signal toward getting a Knowledge Graph panel in Google search (the box that appears on the right side for known entities). The Knowledge Graph is built from structured data, including yours. 4. Local SEO LocalBusiness schema is the single biggest on-page signal for local rankings outside of Google Business Profile. Without it, you are missing a major local ranking factor. The schemas your site probably needs 1. Organization schema (homepage and most other pages) Describes your business as an entity. Goes on the homepage and inherits across the site. Critical fields: name: official business name alternateName: brand variations (rank-day, rank day, etc.) url: canonical homepage URL logo: URL to logo image (recommend 1080×1080 or larger) description: 2 to 3 sentence summary founder: the founder's name foundingDate: year founded sameAs: array of social profile URLs (LinkedIn, Twitter, etc.) contactPoint: phone, email, customer service hours 2. LocalBusiness schema (for businesses with physical locations or service areas) Extends Organization with location-specific fields: address (streetAddress, addressLocality, postalCode, addressCountry) geo (latitude, longitude) openingHoursSpecification (per day) priceRange ($ to $$$$) areaServed (cities or regions served) telephone For specific business types, use the more specific subtype. Dentist instead of LocalBusiness for a dental clinic. LegalService for a law firm. Restaurant for a restaurant. Schema.org has hundreds of specific types. 3. Service schema (for each service page) Apply on each service page describing what that page covers. serviceType: the specific service provider: reference to your Organization areaServed: where the service is available description: what the service includes offers: pricing if applicable 4. FAQPage schema (on FAQ pages and pages with Q&A sections) Marks up question-and-answer content so Google can show the questions as expandable rich results. Each Q&A pair becomes a Question entity with an acceptedAnswer. 5. Article schema (on every blog post) Identifies the page as an article with publication metadata. headline: post title description: post description datePublished, dateModified author: Person object with name publisher: Organization object mainEntityOfPage: URL of the post 6. BreadcrumbList schema (on all interior pages) Shows the page hierarchy. Helps Google display breadcrumb navigation in search results. 7. Product schema (on product or pricing pages) For e-commerce products, but also relevant for service businesses with productised offerings. name, description, image offers (Offer object with price, priceCurrency, availability) aggregateRating (if you have reviews to surface) How to add schema to your site If you use WordPress Plugins handle most schema. Recommended: RankMath (free), Yoast SEO (free), or Schema Pro (paid). Configure them to apply the right schemas to the right page types. Drawback: plugins often inject schema after JavaScript loads, which some tools cannot detect even though Google can read it. If you use Webflow Schema goes in the <head> via custom code per page. Webflow's CMS Collection variables can populate the JSON-LD dynamically. If you use Next.js (like Rankday) Inject JSON-LD via a <script type="application/ld+json"> tag in the layout or page component. Use template strings or JSON.stringify to keep the schema typed and maintainable. If you build manually Add the JSON-LD block to the <head> of each page. Many developers prefer to centralise it in a template that pulls dynamic data per page. How to validate schema Two tools to test your schema: Google Rich Results Test: at search.google.com/test/rich-results. Tells you if your schema is valid and what rich results it qualifies for. Schema.org Validator: at validator.schema.org. Validates schema against the broader specification, not just Google's subset. Run every schema through both. Fix any errors before relying on the markup to drive results. Common schema mistakes Schema doesn't match visible content. If your schema says "ratings: 4.9 stars based on 247 reviews" but those reviews are not visible on the page, Google will eventually penalise you. Wrong type used. Marking a service page as a Product, or vice versa. Use the most specific applicable type. Missing required fields. Some schema types require specific fields. Missing them invalidates the markup. Schema applied to wrong pages. FAQPage schema on a service page that does not have an FAQ section. Article schema on a homepage. Mismatches confuse search engines. Fake reviews schema. Schema marking up reviews that do not actually exist. Detected by Google and results in a manual penalty. Quick start. If your site has zero schema today and you want to deploy the highest-impact 3 schemas, do them in this order: Organization (or LocalBusiness for local businesses), then FAQPage on your FAQ page, then Article on each blog post. That covers 80% of the value. What Rankday does with schema Every Rankday-built site includes the full schema stack from day one: Organization or LocalBusiness on the homepage and layout WebSite schema for brand entity recognition Service schema on each service page FAQPage schema on FAQ pages Article schema on all blog posts BreadcrumbList schema on interior pages Person schema for the founder where appropriate Schema is part of the technical foundation, not an add-on. We apply it before any content goes live so search engines and AI engines understand your business from the first crawl. See the 90-day breakdown or how schema feeds into AI citation. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## SEO for e-commerce stores: the 2026 playbook | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/seo-for-ecommerce-stores

← All posts SEO SEO for e-commerce stores: the 2026 playbook By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 10 min read E-commerce SEO works differently from service business SEO. The site has hundreds or thousands of product pages, dozens of category pages, faceted navigation that can generate millions of URLs, and constant inventory changes. Get the structure wrong and the entire store rankings collapse. Here is the 2026 playbook for ranking an e-commerce store, organised by site element. Product pages Product pages are the bottom of the funnel. They convert traffic into purchases. They need to rank for specific product searches and convert visitors who land on them. Title and meta description Format: [Product Name] - [Key Attribute] | [Brand]. Example: "Nike Air Max 90 - Triple White Leather | Acme Sports." Meta description: 150 to 160 characters describing the product, including price if applicable, key features, and a call to action. Unique product descriptions Manufacturer-provided product descriptions are duplicate content across every retailer selling the same item. Write your own descriptions. Even 100 to 200 words of original copy outranks the duplicated manufacturer text. Product schema Apply Product schema with: name, image, description, brand, sku, gtin (if available), offers (price, currency, availability), aggregateRating (if reviews exist). This is what powers rich results in Google: price, stock status, star ratings appearing directly in search. Customer reviews Product pages with reviews convert 2 to 5x better and rank higher. Build a review collection system that asks customers to review after delivery. Q&A section Common product questions answered on the page. Apply FAQPage schema to mark them up. Gets pulled into rich results and AI Overviews. Out-of-stock handling Do not 404 out-of-stock products. Keep the page indexed with "Out of stock" availability in schema, and offer alternatives or a notify-me option. The page already has accumulated SEO authority. Killing it loses that. Category pages Category pages aggregate products and rank for broader keywords like "men's running shoes" or "leather sofas." They typically drive more traffic than individual product pages. Category description text Add 200 to 500 words of original category description at the top or bottom of the page. Explains what the category contains, helps shoppers choose, includes target keywords naturally. Without this text, category pages are just lists of products, which Google rates as thin. Hierarchy with breadcrumbs Home > Category > Subcategory > Product. Implement BreadcrumbList schema. Helps Google understand site structure and shows breadcrumbs in search results. Internal linking between related categories Link from each category page to related categories. "Customers viewing leather sofas also looked at fabric sofas, ottomans, sofa beds." Helps both users and Google. Faceted navigation: the trap Faceted navigation (filters like color, size, brand, price) can generate millions of URLs. Most are useless duplicates that confuse Google. Strategy: Index strategically valuable facets: "red running shoes," "size 10 sneakers," "Nike running shoes." These have real search demand. Noindex worthless facets: filter combinations with no search demand. Use canonical tags pointing to the parent category. Block crawling of low-value filter combinations: use robots.txt or noindex tags for filter parameters that generate combinatorial URLs. Use clean URLs for valuable facets: /running-shoes/red instead of /running-shoes?color=red&filter=on. Site structure Flat hierarchy Avoid deep nesting. Aim for products to be 2 to 3 clicks from the homepage. Example: Home > Category > Subcategory > Product. HTML sitemap Some e-commerce stores benefit from an HTML sitemap linked in the footer, listing all categories and key products. Aids both discoverability and indexation. XML sitemap segmentation For large stores, segment XML sitemaps by type: product-sitemap.xml, category-sitemap.xml, blog-sitemap.xml. Reference all in a sitemap index. Easier to monitor indexation per segment. Speed and Core Web Vitals E-commerce is unusually demanding for site speed. Image-heavy product pages, third-party scripts (review widgets, chat tools, analytics), and complex checkout flows all slow things down. Priorities: Use WebP or AVIF images with lazy loading Optimise product image sizes (responsive srcset) Defer non-critical scripts Use a CDN for images and static assets Audit third-party scripts ruthlessly. Each one is a tax on speed. Server-side render category and product pages where possible Content beyond products E-commerce sites that only have product and category pages rank narrowly. Adding content layers expands the keyword universe. Buying guides "How to choose [product type]" articles. Educational content that ranks for research-phase keywords. Link to relevant products. Comparison content "[Brand A] vs [Brand B] [product type]." High commercial intent. Comparison articles often outrank individual product pages for comparison queries. Style and use-case content "Best running shoes for marathon training." "Best leather sofas for small apartments." Connects products to specific user contexts. Drives qualified traffic. FAQ pages Common questions about shipping, returns, sizing, payment, materials. Drives long-tail traffic and builds trust. Local SEO for e-commerce If you have a physical store alongside online, treat them separately. Local SEO for the physical store, e-commerce SEO for the online operation. Use LocalBusiness schema for the physical location and Organization schema for the online brand. International SEO for e-commerce If you ship internationally, use hreflang tags or country-specific subdomains/subfolders. Common patterns: example.com/us, example.com/uk, example.com/au (subfolders) us.example.com, uk.example.com (subdomains) example.us, example.co.uk (separate domains) Each version should have unique localized content, prices in local currency, and hreflang tags pointing to the other versions. AI search for e-commerce AI engines like ChatGPT and Perplexity now respond to product recommendation queries. "What's the best running shoe for flat feet?" can return specific product recommendations. To appear in AI recommendations: Apply detailed Product schema Earn editorial product reviews and mentions Build presence on review platforms (Trustpilot, Yotpo, etc.) Publish buying guides that AI engines can pull from Earn third-party comparison content mentioning your products E-commerce SEO is more technical than service SEO because of scale. A 1000-product store has 1000 product pages, dozens of categories, and potentially millions of faceted URLs. Get the structure right or the entire site collapses under crawl budget waste. What Rankday does for e-commerce Our 90-day model is designed primarily for service businesses, but the principles apply to e-commerce. For small-to-medium stores (under 500 SKUs), we can deliver: Full Product and Category schema Category description copywriting Faceted navigation cleanup Buying guide content (4 to 8 pieces) Top product page rewrites Technical SEO foundations For larger e-commerce operations (1000+ SKUs), the work is more involved and usually scoped as a custom engagement. Get in touch via WhatsApp if you want to discuss your store specifically. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## SEO for new domains: what actually works in the first 90 days | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/seo-for-new-domains-first-90-days

← All posts SEO SEO for new domains: what actually works in the first 90 days By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 9 min read Google deliberately holds new domains in a probationary state. The algorithm has not yet collected enough signals to trust them, so they rank slowly for the first 60 to 120 days regardless of how good the on-page work is. Trying to rank for competitive head terms on day 1 of a new domain is a losing strategy. The work that actually moves rankings during the first 90 days is different from the work that moves rankings on an established domain. Here is what to do, sequenced by week. The new-domain reality Google's "trust" of a domain depends on signals it builds over time: How long the domain has existed How much real traffic flows through it (visitors, time on page, return visits) How many other credible domains link to it How consistently the brand appears across third-party sites Whether the content matches user search intent and satisfies queries A 5-day-old domain with perfect on-page SEO has none of these signals yet. A 5-year-old domain with mediocre on-page SEO has many. The difference is months of compounding trust. That does not mean a new domain cannot rank. It means you have to choose your targets carefully and stack the signals fast. What works for new domains in the first 90 days 1. Target long-tail commercial keywords first Head terms ("SEO agency", "best CRM") are dominated by domains that have been working on them for years. A new domain will not crack them in 90 days no matter how much budget you throw at it. Long-tail variants ("fixed-price SEO agency UAE", "best CRM for solo financial advisors") have lower search volume but much lower competition. A new domain can realistically rank top 3 for them within 60 to 90 days. The math is favourable. Ten long-tail terms each driving 20 to 50 monthly visits = 200 to 500 monthly visits with high intent. That outperforms a single head term you cannot actually rank for. 2. Get indexed fast A page that is not indexed cannot rank. Google's first-time crawl of a new domain can take days to weeks. You can accelerate this: Set up Google Search Console immediately Submit your XML sitemap to Search Console Use URL Inspection → Request Indexing for each important page Make sure robots.txt is not accidentally blocking crawlers Ensure every page has a clean canonical tag pointing to itself Add internal links between every page so the crawler can navigate the site 3. Build the citation foundation External signals matter more for new domains than for established ones. The fastest way to build them in the first 30 days is directory citations: Google Business Profile (if applicable) Bing Places, Apple Maps Business LinkedIn company page Industry-specific directories (Clutch, G2, GoodFirms for agencies, etc.) Local directories (Yellow Pages variants, Yelp, Trustpilot) 10 to 20 niche directories specific to your category Each citation is a tiny trust signal. Twenty to thirty of them in the first month build a meaningful foundation that Google notices. 4. Ship 6 to 10 substantial content pages early Each page should be 1500 to 2500 words, target one specific keyword cluster, and provide concrete value. Thin sites with 3 to 5 short pages signal "small, possibly low-quality" to Google. Sites with 6 to 10 substantial pages signal "real business with depth." Aim to have all the priority content live by end of week 4. 5. Apply schema markup site-wide Schema markup is the single highest-leverage technical change for a new domain. It tells search engines and AI engines what your business is, what it does, and where it operates. Without it, Google has to infer everything from prose, which takes longer for an untrusted new domain. Apply: Organization on the homepage and layout, Service on each service page, FAQPage on FAQ pages, Article on all blog posts, LocalBusiness if applicable, BreadcrumbList on interior pages. 6. Optimise Core Web Vitals before launch New domains do not have legacy traffic to "prove" their site speed. Google's first crawl forms the initial impression. If Largest Contentful Paint is over 3 seconds and Cumulative Layout Shift is over 0.25, Google's first impression of your site is "slow." Optimise images, defer non-critical scripts, use a CDN, and use a modern framework (Next.js, Astro, Webflow with care) that ships clean HTML. 7. Start backlink outreach in week 4 Backlinks take longer to build than citations. Start the outreach process early so the first links arrive while the rest of the work is still in progress. Realistic in 90 days: 5 to 15 quality backlinks from outreach (guest posts, resource pages, broken link replacement, HARO mentions). Plus 30 to 50 directory citations. The 90-day sequence Week 1: Foundations Domain set up, hosting configured, SSL active Google Search Console verified, sitemap submitted Google Business Profile created (if applicable), verification started Keyword research complete, target list finalised Site map and information architecture designed Technical SEO checklist applied (robots.txt, canonicals, schema) Week 2: Site live Homepage and 3 to 5 core pages published Schema markup applied to all pages Internal linking structured First 5 directory citations submitted Search Console URL Inspection for all live pages Weeks 3-4: Content depth and citations 3 to 5 more pages published targeting priority long-tail keywords 10 to 15 additional directory citations LinkedIn company page optimised Google Business Profile fully populated with photos, services, Q&A, first posts Initial outreach for first guest post or backlink opportunities Weeks 5-8: Content and signals Total content count reaches 8 to 12 pages Citation count reaches 25 to 40 First 1 to 3 backlinks earned through outreach Search Console shows first rankings appearing (typically week 6 to 8) Review collection system live and producing 1 to 3 reviews per week (for local businesses) Weeks 9-12: Refinement and momentum Rankings start moving for long-tail terms Total backlinks reach 5 to 15 Refine content based on Search Console data (impressions, click-through rates, average position) Double down on pages showing promise, refresh pages stuck below position 10 AI citation tracking starts: query ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude weekly with target queries What does NOT work for new domains Chasing head terms. "Best SEO agency" is impossible for a new domain to rank for in 90 days. Stop trying. Volume-based content strategies. Publishing 50 thin articles in 90 days does not build authority. It signals "AI-generated content farm." Black-hat backlink shortcuts. PBN links, link farms, paid links from "DR 70+ sites" are detected and penalised especially harshly for new domains. Ignoring the technical foundation. A new domain with slow load times, no schema, and broken canonicals has nothing else to fall back on. Changing strategy every 2 weeks. Rankings compound. Constant strategy changes prevent compounding. Realistic expectations. For a new domain in the first 90 days: expect to start ranking for long-tail terms in weeks 6 to 10. Expect top 3 on 60 to 90% of agreed long-tail keywords by day 90. Expect head terms to take 6 to 18 months regardless of effort. The Rankday model for new domains Most of Rankday's clients are on new or recently launched domains. The 90-day playbook is built specifically for that scenario: Realistic keyword scoping (long-tail commercial intent, not head terms) Clean technical foundation from day 1 (no legacy debt to undo) Concentrated citation and content work in the first 6 weeks Outreach for backlinks running in parallel Top 3 rankings on 90% of agreed keywords by day 90, or work continues at no extra cost For new domains, the 90-day model fits the actual constraints of Google's ranking algorithm. See the full breakdown or see the pricing. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## SEO vs AEO vs GEO: what's the difference, and why all three matter | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/seo-vs-aeo-vs-geo

← All posts AEO / GEO SEO vs AEO vs GEO: what's the difference, and why all three matter By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 22, 2026 · 9 min read Five years ago, ranking on Google was the entire game. A buyer searching for a dental clinic, a SaaS tool, or a law firm opened Google, scanned the top three results, and clicked. Today, that same buyer is just as likely to open ChatGPT and ask: "what's the best dental clinic in Dubai that takes walk-ins?" Ranking on Google still matters. It is just no longer the only thing that matters. Three disciplines now compete for the buyer's attention: SEO, AEO, and GEO. They are related, they overlap, and they require different work. Here is how each one differs and why a business that does only SEO in 2026 is losing buyers to competitors who do all three. SEO: Search Engine Optimization SEO is the discipline of ranking on traditional search engines, primarily Google. The goal is to appear in the first three organic results when a buyer searches for a query relevant to your business. That is where roughly 75% of clicks happen. The work is well understood after 20+ years. You write content targeting specific keywords. You earn backlinks from authoritative domains. You structure your site so Google can crawl it efficiently. You optimise for Core Web Vitals so the site loads fast. You apply schema markup so Google understands what each page is about. You build internal linking that signals which pages matter most. SEO is slow. Even for a low-competition keyword, expect 60 to 120 days to break into the top 10. For competitive terms in saturated markets like "best CRM software" or "Dubai real estate agent," six to twelve months of consistent work is normal. What SEO delivers: ranking positions on Google's search results page, organic traffic from those rankings, and the conversions that follow when buyers click through to your site. AEO: Answer Engine Optimization AEO is the discipline of making your business citable by AI assistants. When a user asks ChatGPT "what are the best SEO agencies in the UK," ChatGPT names specific agencies in its answer. AEO is the work that gets your business named in that answer. The mechanics are different from SEO. LLMs do not rank web pages the way Google does. They generate answers from a model trained on structured, credible, consistent information. The signals that matter for AEO are: Structured content. AI engines favour content that directly answers questions. A page that says "We are a dental clinic in Dubai offering implants, cleanings, and orthodontics" is more citable than one that says "We are passionate about smiles." Schema markup. JSON-LD tells AI engines exactly what your business does, who it serves, where it operates, and what it costs. Without schema, an LLM has to guess from your prose. With it, the LLM knows. Directory and review platform presence. LLMs pull heavily from G2, Clutch, Yelp, Trustpilot, TripAdvisor, and category-specific directories. A business missing from those platforms is far less citable than one present on 30 of them. Third-party editorial mentions. A mention in an industry publication, a comparison article, or a credible blog carries weight an LLM was trained to trust. Consistent brand signals. Your business name, address, phone number, and category must match across every platform. Inconsistency is a trust failure that LLMs penalise. AEO is faster than SEO. A business with clean foundations and serious AEO work can start appearing in AI answers within 4 to 12 weeks. What AEO delivers: citations in AI-generated answers across ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Google AI Overviews. The buyer never sees a search results page. They see a single answer that names your business. GEO: Generative Engine Optimization GEO is the newer cousin of AEO. The terms overlap heavily, and many people use them interchangeably. The distinction is subtle but useful. AEO is broader. It covers any AI-driven answer engine, including ones that retrieve and quote sources without generating new prose. GEO is specifically about generative engines: the AI tools that compose answers from your content rather than just quoting it. ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Google AI Overviews all fall in this category. GEO focuses on how those engines synthesise information from multiple sources to construct a single coherent answer, and what you can do to ensure your content gets pulled into that synthesis. The practical work overlaps with AEO almost entirely. Same schema, same directory presence, same structured content. The difference is in how you write the content itself. For GEO specifically, content should be: Decomposable. Each section should answer a single question completely, so a generative engine can lift it without needing the surrounding context. Attributable. Claims should have sources or be specific enough that the engine can attribute them to your brand confidently. Current. Generative engines weight freshness. Content that says "in 2026" or includes recent data outranks content that is undated. Quotable. Short, declarative sentences with concrete facts get pulled into AI answers more than long, hedged paragraphs. Practical rule. If you are writing content with the question "would an AI extract one sentence from this section?" in mind, you are doing GEO. If you are also tracking whether you appear in the answer at all, you are doing AEO. If you are also checking your Google rankings, you are doing SEO. Where the three disciplines overlap The good news is that the work overlaps significantly. A site that does SEO well is roughly 60% of the way to doing AEO and GEO well. The shared foundation is: Clean, fast, mobile-friendly website Clear page structure with proper heading hierarchy Schema markup across all pages Consistent business information across the web High-quality, original content that answers real questions The 40% that is different lives in two areas. First, the kind of content you write. SEO favours longer, comprehensive pages targeting specific keywords. AEO and GEO favour structured answers to specific questions. Second, the kind of external signals you build. SEO needs backlinks. AEO needs directory presence and third-party mentions. Why doing only SEO in 2026 is losing Imagine two competing SEO agencies in Dubai. Both rank on page one of Google for "SEO agency Dubai." A buyer searches that query on Google, sees both, clicks through, and chooses one. Fair fight. Now imagine the same two agencies, but one has done AEO work and the other has not. A different buyer asks ChatGPT "what's the best SEO agency in Dubai?" ChatGPT names one agency confidently. The buyer messages that agency and never opens Google. The agency that ranks on Google but is not cited by ChatGPT lost that buyer before the conversation started. They will never know it happened. ChatGPT does not send referral traffic the way Google does. There is no "users who clicked through from AI search" report. The buyer just shows up at the named business. In 2024, ChatGPT reached 200 million weekly active users. Perplexity handled over 500 million queries per month. Google AI Overviews now sit above organic results for a growing share of commercial queries. The buyer journey has already shifted. The businesses that win in 2026 are the ones whose web presence works for all three: search, AI citation, and generative answers.
