What to do next If you are starting from zero, do them in this order: SEO foundations first, AEO and GEO layered on top. The shared foundation makes all three possible. You cannot do good AEO on a broken website. You cannot get cited by ChatGPT if Google has not crawled you. If you already have a working website with some Google rankings, the priority shifts. AEO and GEO will compound faster than additional SEO investment because most of your competitors are not doing them yet. The window for being one of the first businesses cited by ChatGPT in your category is open for another 12 to 18 months. After that, it closes the way Google SEO closed in 2012. At Rankday we do all three in 90 days for a fixed price. Website rebuild, Google rankings, AI citation. The discipline is integrated because the work is integrated. See the 90-day plan and pricing. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Technical SEO checklist: 21 things to fix before you write a single blog post | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/technical-seo-checklist

← All posts SEO Technical SEO checklist: 21 things to fix before you write a single blog post By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 10 min read Technical SEO is what determines whether Google can crawl, render, and rank your site at all. Get it wrong and the best content strategy on Earth fails. Get it right and the rest of SEO becomes possible. Here is the 21-point checklist to run through before launching any site, or auditing an existing one. Crawlability 1. robots.txt exists and allows crawlers Your robots.txt file sits at /robots.txt and tells crawlers what they can and cannot access. It should exist and not accidentally block important sections. Common mistakes: blocking /assets, /images, or /static folders (which can affect rendering), or forgetting to remove "Disallow: /" left over from staging. 2. XML sitemap exists and is submitted An XML sitemap lists every URL you want indexed. It belongs at /sitemap.xml or referenced in robots.txt. Submit it to Google Search Console and Bing Webmaster Tools. Without submission, discovery takes weeks longer. 3. No accidental noindex tags A meta noindex tag tells search engines not to index a page. Common accident: developers add noindex during staging and forget to remove it on launch. Check every page. The HTML head should not contain <meta name="robots" content="noindex"> unless you actually want the page excluded. 4. Internal links connect every page Every page should be reachable through internal links. Orphan pages (no internal links pointing to them) are rarely crawled. Run a crawler (Screaming Frog) and check the "orphan" report. Fix any. Indexation 5. Canonical URLs set correctly Each page should have a self-referencing canonical tag in the head. The canonical URL should be the version you want indexed (typically https://www. or your chosen primary domain). Common mistakes: canonical pointing to a different domain, canonical pointing to homepage from every page, or no canonical at all. 6. HTTPS and one canonical domain Your site should be served only on HTTPS. HTTP versions should 301-redirect to HTTPS. Non-www and www versions should redirect to whichever you chose as canonical. Test by typing each variant in the browser and confirming it lands on the canonical URL with a single redirect (not a chain). 7. No duplicate content Multiple URLs serving the same content (e.g., /page and /page/, /page?utm=x and /page) confuse Google. Either canonicalise them to one URL or redirect. 8. Pagination handled cleanly For paginated content (blogs, e-commerce category pages), each paginated page should self-canonical and link to the next page. Do not canonical page 2+ to page 1. Site speed and Core Web Vitals 9. Largest Contentful Paint under 2.5 seconds LCP measures when the biggest visible element of your page is rendered. Should be under 2.5 seconds. Common fixes: optimise hero images, defer non-critical JavaScript, use a CDN, choose better hosting. 10. Cumulative Layout Shift under 0.1 CLS measures how much the page jumps around as it loads. Should be under 0.1. Fix by reserving space for images and ads, avoiding late-loading fonts that shift text. 11. Interaction to Next Paint under 200 ms INP measures responsiveness. Replaces the older FID metric. Should be under 200 ms. Fix by reducing JavaScript execution time, splitting large bundles, removing heavy third-party scripts. 12. Modern image formats Use WebP or AVIF instead of JPEG and PNG. Specify width and height attributes on all img tags. Use srcset for responsive images. Lazy-load images below the fold. 13. Compressed text assets Enable gzip or Brotli compression for HTML, CSS, JavaScript. Modern hosts (Vercel, Cloudflare, Netlify) handle this automatically. Mobile and accessibility 14. Mobile-friendly responsive design Google indexes the mobile version of your site. If mobile is broken or worse than desktop, your rankings suffer everywhere. Test with Google's Mobile-Friendly Test tool. 15. Viewport meta tag set Every page should have <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">. Without it, mobile browsers render at desktop width. 16. Tap targets sized correctly Buttons and links on mobile should be at least 48×48 pixels with enough spacing. Cramped targets fail mobile usability checks. Structured data 17. Schema markup applied site-wide Apply Organization on homepage, Service on service pages, Article on blog posts, FAQPage on FAQ sections, LocalBusiness if applicable. Validate with Google's Rich Results Test. 18. Open Graph and Twitter Card metadata Every page should have og:title, og:description, og:image, twitter:card metadata. Controls how the page appears when shared on LinkedIn, X, Slack, WhatsApp. URLs and redirects 19. Clean, readable URLs URLs should be lowercase, hyphenated, descriptive, and short. /seo-for-plumbers is good. /index.php?cat=23&item=789 is bad. 20. No redirect chains Redirects should be single 301s, not chains. Chain example: HTTP → HTTPS → non-www → www. Each hop is a small SEO penalty. Configure your host to do the entire redirect in one step. 21. 404s tracked and fixed Check Search Console's "Pages" report for 404 errors. For each, either restore the page, 301-redirect to a relevant alternative, or accept and leave it (which signals to Google the URL is gone). Quick audit. Open Search Console, run PageSpeed Insights, run Mobile-Friendly Test, run Rich Results Test. In 30 minutes you will have a clear picture of which of these 21 points need attention. Tools to use Google Search Console (free): indexation status, crawl errors, Core Web Vitals, search performance. Google PageSpeed Insights (free): Core Web Vitals scores with specific fix recommendations. Google Rich Results Test (free): validates schema markup. Mobile-Friendly Test (free): mobile usability. Screaming Frog SEO Spider (free up to 500 URLs): site-wide crawl audit. Ahrefs / Semrush (paid): comprehensive technical audits, broken links, redirect chains. What Rankday does Technical SEO is the first work we do in every Rankday engagement. The full 21-point checklist is applied in week 1, before any content goes live. Schema markup, Core Web Vitals optimisation, clean URL structure, canonical setup, and mobile-first rendering are non-negotiable foundations. See the 90-day breakdown. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## What are backlinks, and how do you actually get them in 2026? | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/what-are-backlinks-and-how-to-get-them

← All posts SEO What are backlinks, and how do you actually get them in 2026? By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 9 min read A backlink is a hyperlink from another website pointing to your site. It is the single biggest off-page Google ranking factor and one of the strongest trust signals for AI engines as well. Most SEO agencies are bad at getting backlinks. The strategies that actually work are slow, manual, and require real outreach. The strategies that look fast (link farms, paid link schemes) destroy domains. Here is what good backlink building actually looks like in 2026. Why backlinks still matter Google's ranking algorithm has hundreds of factors, but backlinks remain among the top three for competitive queries. The logic is simple: when one website links to another, it is implicitly endorsing the linked content. Lots of endorsements from credible sources signal that your content is trustworthy. For AI engines, backlinks function as a proxy for credibility. A business mentioned (and linked) in industry publications, comparison sites, and editorial content gets pulled into AI answers more often than one mentioned only on its own website. For brand searches, backlinks help Google understand entity identity. Multiple sites linking to rank-day.com using the anchor text "Rankday" teach Google that those two strings refer to the same entity. What makes a good backlink 1. Relevance A link from a site in your industry or adjacent industries is worth more than a link from an unrelated site. A SEO agency linking to your dental clinic site is less valuable than a healthcare industry publication linking to it. 2. Authority The linking site's own credibility matters. Sites with high Domain Rating, lots of their own backlinks, and editorial reputation pass more authority through their links. Domain Rating (DR) and Domain Authority (DA) are third-party metrics. Anything above DR 50 is generally considered authoritative. The really valuable links come from sites with DR 70+. 3. Editorial placement A link that appears in the editorial body of an article is more valuable than one in a footer, sidebar, or author bio. Google can detect the difference and weights editorial links higher. 4. Anchor text variety The anchor text (the clickable text of the link) matters. Natural backlink profiles include a mix of: Branded anchors ("Rankday", "rank-day.com") Descriptive anchors ("SEO agency in Dubai", "90-day SEO service") Generic anchors ("click here", "this article", "learn more") URL anchors ("https://www.rank-day.com") Over-optimised anchor text (every link uses "SEO agency Dubai" exactly) signals manipulation and can trigger penalties. 5. Dofollow vs nofollow Some links have a "nofollow" attribute, which tells search engines not to pass authority through them. Most social media links, paid links, and user-generated content links are nofollow. Dofollow links pass more authority. But a natural backlink profile has both. All-dofollow profiles look manipulated. How to actually get backlinks in 2026 1. Directories and citations Industry directories and review platforms (Clutch, G2, Trustpilot, Yelp, GoodFirms, DesignRush) provide easy first backlinks. They are nofollow on some platforms and dofollow on others, but they all contribute to consistent NAP signals and discoverability. For a new business, getting listed on 30 to 50 relevant directories is the first phase of backlink building. It is also the foundation for AEO. 2. Guest posts on industry publications Writing a well-researched, valuable article for a publication in your industry, in exchange for a link back to your site, remains one of the most effective tactics. How to do it well: Identify 20 to 30 publications in your industry that accept contributed content Read their existing content. Find topic gaps your expertise can fill. Pitch specific article ideas, not vague offers to write "something" Write the article to their actual quality standards, not to maximise your link insertion Include 1 to 2 natural links to your site in the body, plus the author bio link Expected response rate: 5 to 15%. So pitch 20 to 30 to land 2 to 5 published guest posts per quarter. 3. Resource page link building Many sites maintain "resource" or "useful links" pages relevant to their topic. If your content fits, you can request inclusion. Process: Search "[your topic] resources" or "[your topic] useful links" on Google Identify pages that link to similar content Email the page owner with a polite, specific request to include your resource Make clear why your content adds value to their existing list Response rate: 5 to 10%. Higher quality lists and more specific pitches convert better. 4. Broken link building Find dead links on relevant industry sites. Email the site owner: "Hey, your article links to [X] but that link is dead. I wrote something similar that might be a useful replacement." This works because you are doing the site owner a favour (fixing a broken link) while quietly asking for a link to your content. Response rates are typically higher than cold outreach: 10 to 20%. 5. HARO and similar journalist outreach platforms Help A Reporter Out (HARO) and similar platforms connect journalists with sources. Journalists post requests for expert quotes. If you provide a useful quote, you often get cited (and linked) in the published article. For agency owners and subject matter experts, this is one of the highest-quality backlink sources available. A single mention in a major publication outweighs 50 directory listings. 6. Comparison and review content Comparison articles like "best SEO agencies in Dubai" or "top fit-out contractors UAE" rank well and link to multiple businesses in each list. Getting included in these articles requires either: Direct outreach to the author Being notable enough in your category that the author finds you Paying for placement (which crosses into paid links and should be disclosed) 7. Linkable assets Build content other sites want to link to. Original research, data reports, calculators, comparison tables, and free tools all attract organic backlinks over time. Example: a SEO agency that publishes "Annual State of SEO in the UAE" report with original survey data will earn backlinks from anyone writing about UAE SEO for years afterward. Tactics that destroy domains Some "backlink strategies" look like shortcuts but can result in manual penalties that take months or years to recover from: Private Blog Networks (PBNs): agencies that own a network of low-quality sites and place links from them. Google has been detecting and penalising these for over a decade. Comment spam: automated tools that drop links in blog comments across thousands of sites. Almost always nofollow, harmful, and obvious. Forum signature spam: joining forums purely to add a link to your site in the signature. Detected easily. Bought links from "high-DA" sites: link marketplaces selling placements on credible-looking sites. Google has gotten very good at identifying these patterns. Reciprocal link schemes: "I'll link to you if you link to me" on a large scale. Detected algorithmically. Rule. If a tactic feels like it might be against Google's guidelines, it is. The short-term gains are not worth the long-term penalty risk. The only sustainable backlink strategy is genuinely earning placements through valuable content and real outreach. How many backlinks do you need? It depends on competition. For low-competition local queries, 10 to 30 quality backlinks is often enough to rank top 3. For mid-competition commercial keywords, you typically need 50 to 100. For competitive head terms, hundreds or thousands. Quality > quantity. Ten links from DR 70+ relevant industry sites outweigh 500 links from random low-quality directories. Realistic timeline Backlink building is slow. A focused outreach campaign typically produces: Month 1: 5 to 10 directory citations, 1 to 2 editorial mentions Month 2: 1 to 2 guest posts published, more directory citations Month 3: 2 to 4 guest posts, first organic earned mentions Month 6+: compound growth as your content gets discovered and linked What Rankday does Every Rankday engagement includes 30+ citations and backlinks built during the 90 days. We focus on relevant directories, editorial outreach, and resource page inclusions. No PBNs, no paid link schemes, no spam. The work continues to compound after the 90 days because the links are real placements on credible sites, not rented spots on a link farm. See the full 90-day breakdown. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## What is GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)? | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/what-is-geo-generative-engine-optimization

← All posts AEO / GEO What is GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)? By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 22, 2026 · 7 min read GEO stands for Generative Engine Optimization. It is the practice of structuring your content so generative AI engines pull from it when composing answers to user questions. When someone asks ChatGPT "what are the best CRMs for small B2B teams in 2026?", ChatGPT does not just retrieve and quote sources. It synthesises an answer from many sources, in its own words. GEO is the work that ensures your business gets pulled into that synthesis. Most marketers heard of SEO 20 years ago. AEO (Answer Engine Optimization) is the broader umbrella that includes GEO. The terms overlap, but the distinction matters because the work is slightly different. The difference between AEO and GEO AEO is broader. It covers any AI-driven answer engine, including ones that retrieve sources and surface them in a relatively quoted form. Google AI Overviews, for example, often quote sources directly. GEO is specifically about generative engines. ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, and Google's Gemini all generate answers from a model trained on enormous amounts of content. The engine does not just retrieve and quote. It composes. For practical purposes, the work overlaps almost entirely. The same schema markup, the same directory presence, the same structured content. The difference is in the writing itself: GEO emphasises content that can be lifted as a single coherent statement and woven into a generated answer. How generative engines actually work Understanding what GEO is requires understanding what the engines are doing. A generative engine like ChatGPT receives a query, retrieves relevant context (either from its training data or, in browse mode, from live web search), and uses a large language model to compose an answer. The answer is not a list of links. It is a paragraph or two that synthesises information from many sources into a single response. Three things determine whether your content gets pulled into that answer: Is the engine even aware of your content? If your content is in the training data (or, for browse-enabled queries, in the indexed web), the engine can access it. Is your content relevant to the query? The engine looks for content that semantically matches the question being asked. Is your content structured to be lifted? Generative engines favour content that contains clear, complete answers that can be quoted or paraphrased into the response. The signals that drive GEO 1. Structured, question-shaped content Generative engines favour content that answers a specific question directly. A blog post titled "Some thoughts on CRM software" is less GEO-friendly than one titled "Best CRM software for small B2B teams in 2026." Within the content, each section should answer one question completely. The engine should be able to lift a single paragraph and have it stand alone as a useful answer. 2. Concrete facts, not vague claims "Our CRM is fast" is not GEO-friendly. "Our CRM loads in 1.2 seconds on average, measured across 10,000 customer accounts" is. Generative engines prefer to reproduce concrete facts because hallucinations are easier to avoid when the source is explicit. If you want your content quoted, give the engine something specific to quote. 3. Schema markup JSON-LD schema markup describes your page in a machine-readable way. For GEO specifically, the schemas that matter most are Organization, Service, FAQPage, Article, Person, and Product. Without schema, the engine has to infer everything from prose. With schema, it has structured fields it can confidently use in answers. 4. Authority signals Generative engines weight sources by how authoritative they appear. The signals are similar to SEO: backlinks from credible domains, mentions in major publications, presence on trusted directories. A business mentioned in 30 industry directories and a few editorial articles will get pulled into more answers than one mentioned only on its own site. 5. Freshness Many generative engines are trained on data up to a specific cutoff date, plus live web access for current queries. Content that is dated and current ranks higher in generative answers than content that is undated or stale. Including the current year, recent statistics, and recently updated dates in your content all signal to the engine that the content is current. 6. Brand consistency Your business name, category, and core facts must be identical across every platform. Inconsistency confuses the engine and reduces the chance it confidently names you in an answer. What GEO is not Not keyword stuffing Some early "GEO experts" recommend cramming your content with semantic variations of your target query. This does not work. Generative engines are not bag-of-words systems. They understand meaning, and they penalise content that reads like it was written to game the system. Write naturally. Answer the question well. Structure it cleanly. That is GEO. Not just "writing for AI" Some agencies sell "GEO services" that amount to writing slightly different blog posts. That misses the point. GEO is a full discipline that requires schema work, directory presence, third-party mentions, and consistent brand signals across the web, in addition to good writing. Writing alone is roughly 30% of GEO. The other 70% is technical and off-site work. Not a replacement for SEO GEO works alongside SEO, not instead of it. Most of the foundational work (clean site, schema, content depth) overlaps. The difference is in how you write and where you build external signals. Simple test for whether your content is GEO-ready. Ask ChatGPT or Perplexity a question your content is supposed to answer. If your content is in the answer, you are doing GEO. If your competitor's content is in the answer, you have work to do. How to start doing GEO Working from the foundation up: Schema markup. Apply Organization, Service, FAQPage, Article schema across your site. This is the highest-leverage technical change. Restructure content into question-answer format. Each page or section should answer one specific question completely. Add concrete facts. Replace vague claims with specific numbers, dates, prices, and outcomes. Get listed on the directories generative engines pull from. G2, Clutch, GoodFirms, Trustpilot, Yelp, industry-specific platforms. Earn third-party mentions. Comparison articles, industry publications, podcast appearances. These feed into the engine's understanding of who you are. Test regularly. Ask ChatGPT and Perplexity your target queries weekly. Track whether you appear. Why GEO matters in 2026 In 2024, ChatGPT reached 200 million weekly active users. Perplexity handled over 500 million queries per month. Google AI Overviews now appear above traditional organic results for a growing share of commercial queries. The buyer's journey has already shifted. A meaningful share of searches that used to drive Google clicks now end with an AI-generated answer that names a specific business. If your business is not that business, you lost the buyer without ever seeing them. The window for being one of the first in your category to be cited by generative engines is roughly 12 to 18 months. The same window that closed for SEO around 2012, when early movers locked in the rankings that still drive most of their organic traffic today. Rankday includes GEO in every engagement. Schema, structured content, directory placement, and editorial outreach are all part of the standard 90-day playbook. See how we get businesses cited by AI engines. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## What is Local SEO, and does your business actually need it? | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/what-is-local-seo

← All posts SEO What is Local SEO, and does your business actually need it? By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 8 min read Local SEO is the discipline of ranking in the searches buyers run when they want a business near them. If a customer ever searches "near me," includes a city or neighbourhood in their query, or asks ChatGPT for recommendations in a specific area, local SEO is the work that decides whether your business shows up. For some categories, local SEO drives more leads than the entire website combined. For others, it is irrelevant. Here is how to tell which category you fall into, and what to actually do about it. What "local" means to Google in 2026 When a user runs a search that Google detects as having local intent, the results page changes. Instead of just blue links, the user sees: The local pack (sometimes called the map pack): 3 business listings with a map, often above the organic results. Google Business Profile listings for each of those 3 businesses, with reviews, photos, hours, and a directions button. Organic results below the local pack, often filtered toward businesses with local relevance. For commercial local queries, the local pack often gets more clicks than the entire organic results section. If you are not in the top 3 there, you are largely invisible. Does your business need local SEO? Local SEO is critical if your business serves customers in a specific geographic area and they search for businesses like yours. Categories where local SEO is essential: Clinics, dental practices, aesthetic clinics, GPs, specialists Law firms, accountants, financial advisors Restaurants, hotels, gyms, salons, spas Plumbers, electricians, contractors, home services Retail stores, real estate brokers, dealerships Local B2B service providers Categories where local SEO is less critical or irrelevant: Pure online businesses serving customers globally Most SaaS companies E-commerce stores shipping nationwide or internationally Drop-shipping or print-on-demand businesses Online courses, digital products, content publishers Even for online businesses, local SEO occasionally matters. A SaaS company headquartered in Dubai that wants to be cited as "the best [category] tool in the UAE" benefits from local relevance signals. But for most pure-online businesses, traditional SEO and AEO are higher priorities. The three core elements of local SEO 1. Google Business Profile Your Google Business Profile (formerly Google My Business) is the single most important element of local SEO. It is the listing that shows up in the local pack and on Google Maps. A complete profile includes: Verified business location (or service area if you do not have a physical location customers visit) Accurate business name, address, phone number (must match your website exactly) Primary category and all relevant secondary categories Business hours including special hours for holidays Photos: exterior, interior, team, products, service in action Services list with descriptions and pricing where appropriate Products if applicable Q&A section with proactively answered common questions Posts: weekly updates, offers, events (similar to social media posts but for Google) Active review collection and response strategy Most businesses set up the basics and stop there. The competitive advantage comes from the elements most businesses skip: weekly posts, proactive Q&A, consistent review responses, photo updates. 2. Citations and NAP consistency NAP stands for Name, Address, Phone. Google uses NAP consistency across the web as a trust signal. If your business name is "Acme Dental" on Google but "Acme Dental Clinic LLC" on Yelp and "Acme Dental Practice" on Yellow Pages, Google does not know which to trust. Citations are mentions of your business on directories, industry sites, and review platforms. Each citation should use the exact same NAP information. Inconsistency degrades local rankings. The minimum citation set for most local businesses: Google Business Profile Bing Places Apple Maps Business Yelp (or local equivalent: Yellow Pages UAE, Yell.com, etc.) Facebook Business Page LinkedIn company page 5 to 10 industry-specific directories 5 to 10 local directories specific to your city 3. Local content and on-page signals Your website needs to signal local relevance. Specifically: The city or area name in titles, H1s, and meta descriptions for relevant pages LocalBusiness schema markup with structured address, geo coordinates, opening hours, and service area An embedded Google Map on the contact page or footer Location-specific pages if you serve multiple areas (a dedicated page per city or neighbourhood) Customer testimonials or case studies from local customers Content mentioning local landmarks, events, or context where natural Reviews: the local SEO multiplier Reviews are the single biggest local SEO ranking factor outside the basic Google Business Profile setup. They affect: Whether you appear in the local pack at all Your position within the local pack Click-through rate from search to your profile Conversion rate from profile view to call or visit Whether AI engines cite you when asked for recommendations Effective review collection looks like: A simple link to your Google Review form, shared after every transaction or service SMS or email follow-up 1 to 3 days after the service A printable QR code at the point of sale for in-person businesses Responding to every review (positive and negative) within 48 hours Avoiding fake or paid reviews, which Google detects and penalises Common local SEO mistakes Multiple Google Business Profiles for the same location. Causes duplicate listings and confusion. Consolidate to one verified listing. Wrong primary category. Choose the category that most closely matches your core service. Wrong category = ranking for the wrong queries. Service area too broad. Listing every city within 100 miles dilutes your relevance for the city you actually serve. Be honest about your service area. Inconsistent NAP across the web. Audit and standardise. Ignoring negative reviews. A non-response looks worse than a polite acknowledgment. Always respond. No photos, or only stock photos. Profiles with real photos get more clicks. Real photos beat stock by a wide margin. Quick local SEO health check. Search your business name on Google. Then search "[your category] [your city]" on Google. Then search both queries on ChatGPT. If you do not show up clearly in all four, you have local SEO work to do. Local SEO and AEO together Increasingly, local queries are happening on AI assistants too. A buyer asking ChatGPT "what's a good dentist in Business Bay Dubai" is doing the same job as a Google search, but the answer comes from a different signal chain. For AI engines to recommend your local business, they need consistent NAP signals across the directories they were trained on, structured content explaining what you do and where, and ideally third-party mentions in local publications. The work overlaps with traditional local SEO almost entirely. The difference is that the citation and consistency work matters more for AI than the on-page work does. Where Rankday handles local SEO Every Rankday engagement for a local business includes a Google Business Profile rebuild, citation network setup (30+ relevant directories), review collection system, and LocalBusiness schema markup. For multi-location businesses, we build dedicated location pages targeting each city or neighbourhood you serve. See how we handle Dubai local SEO or see the full pricing. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Why did my Google ranking drop? A diagnostic and recovery playbook | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/why-did-my-google-ranking-drop

← All posts SEO Why did my Google ranking drop? A diagnostic and recovery playbook By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 23, 2026 · 9 min read A sudden ranking drop is one of the most stressful things in SEO. Traffic falls. Phones go quiet. You did not change anything (you think) and yet position 3 has become position 17 overnight. Panicking does not help. Working through a diagnostic in order does. Here is the order, with the recovery playbook for each cause. The diagnostic order 1. Verify the drop is real Before you fix anything, confirm the drop actually happened. Rankings fluctuate normally by 1 to 3 positions day to day. A drop of 1 to 2 spots that lasts 2 days is noise, not a problem. Open Google Search Console > Performance. Set the date range to the last 90 days. Filter by the affected query or page. If the drop is real and persistent, it will show clearly. Also: check if the drop is on one keyword or many. One keyword = page-level issue. Many keywords across the site = site-wide issue. 2. Check the date the drop started The exact date matters. Google rolls out algorithm updates on specific days. If your drop matches a known update date, you have your likely cause. Check Google's official algorithm updates page or sites like Search Engine Land's "Google algorithm updates" tracker. Cross-reference your drop date with their list. If the date matches an algorithm update, the cause is likely one of: content quality, E-E-A-T, helpful content, spam, or core update. 3. Check for site changes Almost every "we didn't change anything" drop turns out to involve a change. Investigate: Did you redesign or relaunch the site recently? Did you migrate to a new platform? Did you change URLs, even slightly? Did you install or remove a plugin? Did you change hosting? Did you update content on the affected pages? Did you change meta titles or descriptions? Even small changes can have large effects. A URL change without redirects, a plugin that injects noindex tags, a theme update that breaks schema. All cause sudden drops. 4. Check Search Console for manual actions Search Console > Security & Manual Actions > Manual Actions. If there is anything here other than "No issues detected," you have a manual penalty from Google's team. Manual actions are rare but devastating. They almost always require you to fix the underlying issue (spammy links, hacked content, deceptive practices) and submit a reconsideration request. 5. Check indexation status Search Console > Pages. Make sure the affected pages are still indexed. Sometimes pages drop out of the index entirely. Common causes: accidental noindex tag, removed from sitemap, blocked by robots.txt change, canonical pointing elsewhere. 6. Check Core Web Vitals If Core Web Vitals scores tanked recently, rankings can drop. Common cause: heavy new scripts added (ads, chat widgets, analytics), unoptimized images, slow hosting after traffic spike. 7. Check backlinks Look at your backlink profile in Ahrefs or Search Console > Links. Did you lose major backlinks recently? Did spammy backlinks appear (possibly a negative SEO attack)? Lost authority backlinks can cause ranking drops. Sudden influx of low-quality backlinks can trigger a penalty. 8. Check competitor activity Sometimes you did not drop. Your competitors rose. Look at who is now above you. Did they publish new content? Earn new backlinks? Launch a new product page? If competitors are now better optimised, you need to respond by improving your own content depth, internal linking, or backlinks. 9. Check search intent shift Google sometimes reinterprets search intent. A query that used to favour service pages might now favour comparison content. If the SERP for your target keyword has shifted (different result types now dominate), your page format may no longer match intent. Run the query yourself. What format are the top 3 results now? If different from before, your page may need a structural change. Recovery playbooks by cause Core algorithm update Google's core updates re-evaluate the entire algorithm's calibration. Sites with weak E-E-A-T, thin content, or low user satisfaction often drop. Sites with strong fundamentals often rise. Recovery: improve content quality, add expert author attribution, deepen pages with original insight, earn authority signals. Recovery from core updates typically takes 3 to 6 months because you have to wait for the next update to incorporate your improvements. Helpful Content update Targets sites that mass-produce thin, AI-written, or low-effort content designed to rank rather than to help users. Recovery: audit your content. Delete or rewrite the weakest 20%. Focus on demonstrating real experience and expertise. Cut AI-generated filler. Spam update Targets sites with manipulative practices: link schemes, cloaking, scraped content, doorway pages. Recovery: clean up the practices. Disavow spammy backlinks. Delete or rebuild compromised content. Submit a reconsideration request if you have a manual action. Site migration without redirects Common cause: changed URL structure or moved to a new domain without 301 redirects. Recovery: set up 301 redirects from old URLs to new ones. Update internal links to point to new URLs. Resubmit sitemap to Search Console. Recovery typically 4 to 8 weeks. Lost backlinks Recovery: identify which backlinks were lost. Reach out to the linking sites to restore. Build new backlinks to replace lost authority. Slow process. Competitor improvement Recovery: match or exceed the competitor's content. Improve your internal linking. Earn new backlinks. Update content with fresher data. Search intent shift Recovery: rewrite the page to match the new dominant intent format. If the SERP now favours listicles, rewrite as a listicle. If it favours comparisons, add comparison content. First principle. Do not panic-make changes during a ranking drop. Diagnose first. Random changes during a drop often make things worse. Identify the cause, then execute the specific recovery playbook. Realistic recovery timelines Technical issue (noindex, broken redirect, blocked crawler): 1 to 3 weeks after fixing. Lost backlinks: 4 to 12 weeks to rebuild authority. Content quality issue: 2 to 6 months. Requires next algorithm update to fully recover. Manual action: 4 to 8 weeks after a successful reconsideration request, but only after the underlying issue is genuinely fixed. Core algorithm update: 3 to 9 months. You have to wait for the next core update to incorporate your improvements. Preventing future drops Monitor Search Console weekly. Catch drops early before they compound. Document every site change. Keep a log of redesigns, migrations, plugin installs. When a drop happens, you can correlate with changes. Maintain content quality. Audit and refresh older pages quarterly. Delete or rebuild weak content. Build diverse backlinks. Do not rely on one or two big backlinks. Diversify across many credible sources. Build other channels. Direct traffic, email, social, AI search citations all reduce dependence on any single algorithm. What Rankday does We monitor Search Console weekly for every active client. Sudden drops trigger an immediate diagnostic before any reactive changes. The 90-day playbook also builds resilience: clean technical foundation, real content, diverse backlinks, AI citation work. Sites with this foundation drop less often and recover faster. If you are dealing with a ranking drop now, the first step is the diagnostic above. Get in touch via WhatsApp on the FAQ page if you want a specific opinion on what to do. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Why isn't my website ranking on Google? 9 most common reasons | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/why-isnt-my-website-ranking-on-google

← All posts SEO Why isn't my website ranking on Google? 9 most common reasons By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 22, 2026 · 9 min read If your business is not on the first page of Google for the searches your buyers run, the cause is almost always one of nine specific reasons. Most are fixable in under 30 days. Here is the diagnostic order from most common to least common. Work through them in sequence. The earlier reasons are the most likely culprits, and fixing them often resolves the issue without needing to investigate further. 1. Your site is not indexed If Google has not added your pages to its index, you cannot rank for anything. This is the most common cause and the easiest to miss. How to check: search site:yourdomain.com in Google. If you see no pages or far fewer than your site contains, you have an indexing problem. Then open Google Search Console, go to Pages, and look at the indexing report. You will see pages in three categories: indexed, crawled-but-not-indexed, and discovered-but-not-crawled. Common fixes: submit the sitemap to Search Console, use URL Inspection to manually request indexing for each page, ensure robots.txt is not blocking crawlers, ensure no pages have noindex tags applied accidentally. 2. Your domain is too new Google deliberately holds new domains in a probationary state. Even with perfect setup, a domain less than 6 months old will rank slowly for anything competitive. How to check: when was the domain registered? If under 6 months, this is likely part of your problem. Fix: time. Plus the work below to accelerate trust signals: external mentions, citations, backlinks, and content that genuinely helps users. 3. Your content does not match search intent Each Google query has an implicit intent. Buyers searching "best CRM for B2B" want a comparison article, not a product page. Buyers searching "CRM pricing" want to see prices, not a marketing pitch. How to check: search your target keyword. Look at the top 3 organic results. What format are they? Comparison post? Listicle? Product page? Service page? Local result? If your page is a different format from what is ranking, you have a search intent mismatch. Either change your page format to match, or target a different keyword. 4. Your content is too thin or too generic Google now strongly penalises thin content. A 200-word page does not rank for a competitive keyword in 2026. A page with no original insight, no specific data, and no clear value to the reader will not rank either. How to check: pick your top ranking page. Read it next to the top 3 ranking competitors. Is yours less detailed, less specific, less helpful? If yes, content depth is your problem. Fix: rewrite or extend the page. Aim for 1500 to 2500 words with concrete examples, specific data, original insight, and clear answers to the question the user is asking. 5. No backlinks pointing to the page For competitive keywords, content quality alone is not enough. Google needs external signals that other sites consider your content trustworthy. Those signals come from backlinks: other domains linking to your page. How to check: use a free tool like Ahrefs Backlink Checker, Moz, or SE Ranking to see how many backlinks point to your domain. If the answer is "fewer than 10" and you are trying to rank for anything competitive, this is part of the problem. Fix: earn backlinks through editorial outreach, guest posts on credible industry publications, citations on relevant directories, and PR. Avoid link farms and paid link schemes, which can permanently damage your domain. 6. Your site has technical SEO problems Common technical problems that block ranking: Slow page load. Pages that take 5+ seconds to load rank below faster competitors. Aim for Largest Contentful Paint under 2.5 seconds. Mobile usability issues. Google indexes mobile-first. If your site is broken on mobile, you will rank worse on every device. Missing or wrong schema markup. Schema does not directly affect ranking but enables rich results that drive higher click-through rates. Broken internal links. 404 errors and redirect loops confuse crawlers and reduce indexability. Duplicate content. Multiple pages targeting the same keyword cause keyword cannibalisation. Improper canonicalisation. If your canonical tags point to the wrong URLs, Google may index the wrong page or none at all. How to check: run the site through Google PageSpeed Insights and Search Console's Core Web Vitals report. Also use a crawler like Screaming Frog to spot internal link issues and duplicate content. 7. Keyword competition is too high Some keywords are dominated by domains that have invested years and millions of dollars in SEO. Trying to outrank them with limited resources is a losing strategy. How to check: search your target keyword. Look at the domains ranking in the top 10. Are they enterprise-scale brands, government sites, major publications, or industry giants? If yes, you are competing with very deep pockets. Fix: target less competitive long-tail keywords first. "Best CRM" is extremely competitive. "Best CRM for solo financial advisors in the UK" is significantly more achievable. Build authority on long-tails first, then climb up. 8. Your title and meta descriptions are bad Even if you rank, a bad title and meta description tank your click-through rate. Google notices low CTR and lowers your ranking further over time. How to check: search your target keyword. Find your result. Is the title compelling? Does it match the user's intent? Does the meta description give a clear reason to click? Common mistakes: duplicate titles across pages, titles that do not include the target keyword, generic descriptions like "We are the best SEO agency in town." Fix: unique title and meta description per page, primary keyword in title, compelling reason to click in meta description. 9. Google has manually penalised your site Rare but real. If your site has a history of spammy backlinks, hacked content, or violation of Google's guidelines, you may have a manual action against your domain. How to check: Search Console → Security & Manual Actions → Manual actions. If you see anything other than "No issues detected," you have a manual penalty. Fix: depends on the specific violation. For unnatural backlinks, disavow them and submit a reconsideration request. For content violations, fix the content and submit a reconsideration request. The process takes weeks to months. Diagnostic order. Start with #1 (indexation) and work through in order. Most "my site isn't ranking" issues are resolved in the first 3 reasons. Reasons 4 through 9 are progressively rarer and more involved to fix. The 30-day diagnostic Here is a 30-day plan to diagnose and fix most ranking problems: Days 1-2: verify indexation. Check site: searches and Search Console. Submit sitemap. Request indexing for any unindexed key pages. Days 3-7: audit search intent. For each target keyword, compare your page format to top 3 ranking results. Note mismatches. Days 8-14: rewrite or extend thin content. Pick the 3 most important pages, get each to 1500+ words with concrete value. Days 15-21: technical audit. Run PageSpeed Insights, Search Console, and a crawler. Fix the obvious issues. Days 22-30: begin backlink outreach. Identify 10 relevant industry publications, pitch guest posts or inclusion in their roundups. After 30 days, the most common ranking problems are either fixed or clearly identified. The longer-term work (building backlinks and earning citations) continues from there. When you need help Some of this work is straightforward. Some requires deeper expertise: schema markup, content strategy, backlink outreach, technical site rebuilds. Rankday does a complete 90-day audit and rebuild for businesses whose current setup is not delivering. Standard $4,900. Top 3 ranking guarantee on agreed keywords by day 90, or we keep working at no extra cost. See the full pricing or how the 90 days work. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days

---

## Will AI replace Google search? The honest answer for 2026 | Rankday
URL: https://www.rank-day.com/blog/will-ai-replace-google-search

← All posts AEO / GEO Will AI replace Google search? The honest answer for 2026 By Ijas Abdulla · MAY 22, 2026 · 8 min read Short answer: not entirely, but the shift has already happened for a large share of queries that used to be Google's domain. Google still handles billions of queries a day. But the queries that move money in 2026 are increasingly happening on AI assistants instead. Here is what the data shows, what the shift means for your traffic, and how to adapt. Where AI has already won Some categories of queries have shifted decisively to AI. Buyers no longer Google these. Comparison and recommendation queries "What's the best CRM for a 20-person sales team?" used to be a query that drove Google traffic to comparison articles, G2 listings, and SaaS company landing pages. Today, that question is more often asked of ChatGPT, which returns a direct answer naming specific tools. The buyer who would have clicked through 5 comparison pages 3 years ago now reads one ChatGPT answer and shortlists 2 to 3 tools to investigate further. How-to and explanation queries "How do I configure SPF records?" "What's the difference between TypeScript and JavaScript?" These used to drive billions of clicks to Stack Overflow, MDN, and dev blogs. Many of those queries now resolve in ChatGPT without the user ever opening a search engine. Research and learning queries Students, researchers, and professionals doing background reading on a topic. ChatGPT and Claude have replaced the first three to five hours of Google research for a meaningful share of these users. Where Google still dominates Transactional and local queries "Plumber near me." "Best dental clinic Dubai." These still happen on Google. The buyer wants a Maps result or a Google Business Profile listing, not a generated paragraph. Caveat: AI is encroaching here too. ChatGPT can answer "best dental clinic in Dubai" with named recommendations. But Google still wins for "near me" queries that require location-aware results. Brand and navigational queries "Rankday pricing." "Notion login." Users who already know what they want and just need to get there still type the brand name into Google. AI does not replace this. News and very current information Live sports scores, breaking news, today's weather. Google still dominates. ChatGPT without browse is limited by training data cutoff. ChatGPT with browse is improving but still slower than a Google search for these queries. Visual queries Anything that requires images, videos, or maps. Google still wins because the interface returns rich media. AI assistants are catching up but text-first remains the default. The hybrid: Google AI Overviews Google itself has embedded AI directly into search. Google AI Overviews appear above traditional organic results for a growing share of commercial queries. The result is a hybrid: traditional Google search at the bottom of the page, AI-generated overview at the top. For your site, this is a double-edged sword. If your content is the source the AI Overview pulls from, you might get more visibility. If your content is below the Overview, your click-through rate drops because users get the answer without scrolling. Early data suggests AI Overviews reduce click-through rates by 30 to 60% on queries where they appear. For informational queries, that is a major change in how organic traffic flows. What the data actually shows Reliable data on this shift is limited (the AI assistants do not publish detailed query data), but a few signals are clear: ChatGPT had 200+ million weekly active users in 2024. A meaningful portion of those interactions are queries that would otherwise have been Googled. Perplexity grew from a few million queries per month to over 500 million per month in 18 months. Almost all of that growth is queries diverted from traditional search. Google's response was to embed AI into search itself. Google AI Overviews now run on a meaningful share of commercial queries. Click-through rates on top organic results have declined in the categories where AI Overviews appear. The traffic has not disappeared, but it is being filtered through AI summaries. Bottom line so far. AI has not replaced Google. AI has shifted the kinds of queries Google handles, and Google has responded by becoming partly AI itself. The net effect is that "rank on Google" in 2026 means something different from what it meant in 2020. What this means for your business Organic traffic from Google will keep declining for informational queries If your content strategy depends on driving traffic to informational blog posts, expect that traffic to decline as AI Overviews and AI assistants pull the answers directly. The way to maintain visibility is to be the source the AI pulls from, not just to rank below the AI summary. Commercial and brand queries still drive Google traffic Buyers looking to purchase, compare specific brands, or navigate to a known business still go to Google. SEO for these queries continues to work and will keep working. AEO and GEO are no longer optional Optimising for AI engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude, Google AI Overviews) is now a real channel. Businesses that get cited in AI answers reach buyers that businesses doing only SEO will miss entirely. See our breakdown of SEO vs AEO vs GEO for the full distinction. The window for early movers is closing Like every search shift before, the businesses that adapt early lock in the visibility. The early ChatGPT citations and AI Overview features will solidify into a stable set of named businesses, the same way Google rankings did in the early 2010s. Realistically, the window for being one of the first cited businesses in your category is 12 to 18 months. After that, displacing the incumbents is much harder. How to adapt Practical steps to maintain visibility as the shift continues: Keep your SEO foundation strong. The technical site work that powers Google rankings also powers AI citation. Do not abandon SEO. Add structured content. Each page should answer specific questions completely, in a way that can be lifted into an AI answer. Apply schema markup site-wide. JSON-LD is how AI engines understand what your business is. Build presence on the directories AI engines pull from. G2, Clutch, Trustpilot, GoodFirms, plus category-specific platforms. Earn third-party mentions. Comparison articles, industry publications, podcasts. These are the sources AI engines learned to trust. Test weekly. Ask ChatGPT and Perplexity your target queries. Track whether you appear. Iterate. The honest answer AI has not replaced Google. The total search market is still dominated by Google. But AI has changed which queries flow through Google, and Google has changed itself to integrate AI directly. The result is a search ecosystem where: Informational queries increasingly resolve in AI assistants or AI Overviews Commercial queries still drive Google clicks, but click-through rates depend on whether AI Overviews show up Brand and navigational queries are largely unchanged Comparison and recommendation queries have shifted significantly to AI For most businesses, the practical answer is: keep doing SEO, but layer AEO and GEO on top. Doing only SEO in 2026 means missing the channels where many of your buyers now start their research. Rankday does all three in one engagement. Website, Google rankings, AI citations. See the 90-day plan. Ready to rank? Start your 90 days
